cobo.original: Hay en estas sierras un árbol llamado de los indios quina-quina, de cuya corteza se hace el polvo que tanto renombre ha ganado en Europa para las tercianas y cuartanas.
cobo.translation: In these mountains there is a tree called by the Indians quina-quina, from whose bark is made the powder that has gained such renown in Europe for tertian and quartan fevers. The Indians had used it from time immemorial for the fevers they call chuccho.
cobo.preparation: Bark ground to powder, dissolved in wine or water
cobo.notes: Cobo personally transported cinchona bark to Europe in 1632.

modern_research.summary: Quinine isolated 1820. Still first-line treatment for severe malaria. WHO Essential Medicine.
modern_research.significance: One of the most consequential pharmacological discoveries in history.

plants.evidence_authority: Quinine — FDA approved antimalarial; quinidine FDA approved antiarrhythmic

cobo.original: La hoja de coca es una de las más preciosas cosas que tiene este reino del Perú. Los indios la mastican mezclándola con cal. Úsanla también para el dolor de muelas y cabeza.
cobo.translation: The coca leaf is one of the most precious things in Peru. The Indians chew it mixed with lime, drawing great vigor. They also use it for toothache and headache, applying it to the painful area.
cobo.preparation: Leaves chewed with alkaline substance; applied topically for pain
cobo.condition: Altitude sickness, fatigue, dental and head pain, anesthesia
cobo.notes: Cobo's description of topical anesthetic use predates cocaine isolation by two centuries.

modern_research.summary: Cocaine first local anesthetic (1884). All subsequent local anesthetics derive from cocaine's mechanism.
modern_research.significance: Transformed surgery. The topical pain application Cobo documents is precisely the mechanism of local anesthesia.

plants.evidence_authority: Cocaine HCl — FDA approved topical anesthetic (ENT surgery)
plants.evidence_note: Only legal medical use; ecgonine esters also pharmaceutical

badianus.original: Ad morbum gallicum: Herbae ololiuhqui semen, atque oleum ex eo expressum, cum lapide tizatl et alba terra mixtum, atque calefactum, corpori illiniatur.
badianus.translation: For the French disease: The seed of ololiuhqui and the oil pressed from it, mixed with tizatl stone and white earth, heated and applied to the body. Pain is immediately calmed.
badianus.preparation: Seeds ground, oil extracted, mixed with mineral compounds applied topically

florentine.original: In ololiuhqui: tlanelhuayo, tlazo. Ic tlapaloa, ic patia.
florentine.translation: The ololiuhqui: it has roots, it is precious. With it one gives courage, with it one is healed.

modern_research.summary: Albert Hofmann identified ergine in ololiuqui seeds 1960. Anti-inflammatory properties under investigation.
modern_research.significance: Cross-source validation between Badianus and Florentine Codex.

plants.evidence_authority: Ergine (LSA) — Schedule I; psilocybin trials ongoing; no approval
plants.priority_reason: 7 independent civilisations independently documented this · condition burden is critical · compound identified but no clinical trials

badianus.original: Ad tumores et dolores articulorum: Herbae toloatzin folia in oleo cocta et calida parti affectae applicentur. Cave ne nimis utaris: venenum est si plus quam par est sumitur.
badianus.translation: For swellings and joint pains: The leaves of toloatzin, cooked in oil and applied warm to the affected part. Take care not to use too much: it is a poison if taken in more than the proper amount.
badianus.preparation: Leaves cooked in oil, applied warm topically
badianus.condition: Joint pain, swelling, topical pain management
badianus.notes: Dosage warning demonstrates understanding of therapeutic window.

florentine.original: Toloatzin: xochitl, patli. Zan quezqui ic patia: miac ic miqui.
florentine.translation: Toloatzin: flower, medicine. Only a little heals: too much causes death.
florentine.notes: Identical dosage warning to Badianus — two independent sources, same precise clinical observation.

modern_research.summary: All three alkaloids are WHO Essential Medicines. Scopolamine: FDA-approved for nausea. Atropine: cardiac arrest, nerve agent treatment.
modern_research.significance: EXCEPTIONAL CONVERGENCE: Both sources document identical use AND identical dosage warning.

plants.evidence_authority: Scopolamine — FDA approved (Transderm Scop patch, motion sickness/nausea); atropine FDA approved; hyoscyamine FDA approved
plants.evidence_note: WHO Essential Medicine — atropine on EML

cobo.original: El paico se halla en todas partes de este reino. Los indios la usan grandemente para las lombrices.
cobo.translation: The paico is found in all parts of this kingdom. The Indians use it greatly for worms in children and adults.

badianus.original: Ad vermes intestinorum: Herbae epazotl sucum cum aqua tepida bibe. Vermes expellit cito.
badianus.translation: For intestinal worms: Drink the juice of epazotl with warm water. It expels worms quickly.

florentine.original: Epazotl: patli, qualli patli. Ic quixtia in coatl.
florentine.translation: Epazotl: medicine, good medicine. With it the worm is expelled.

modern_research.summary: Ascaridole has well-documented anthelmintic activity. Renewed interest as drug-resistant parasites emerge.
modern_research.significance: MAXIMUM CONVERGENCE: All three sources confirm identical antiparasitic application across two continents.

plants.evidence_authority: Ascaridole — not approved; antiparasitic research ongoing
plants.priority_reason: 10 independent civilisations independently documented this · condition burden is critical · compound identified but no clinical trials

ayurveda.original: Sarpagandha — sarpa-gandha — tasya mūlam viṣaghnam śirorogaharam nidrakaraṃ ca
ayurveda.translation: Sarpagandha — named for its snake-like smell — its root is antidote to poison, remedy for diseases of the head, and inducer of sleep. Used for madness (unmada), for venomous creature bites, and for the fever of those who cannot rest.
ayurveda.preparation: Root dried and powdered; taken in milk or water
ayurveda.notes: Documented in Ayurvedic texts for over 3,000 years specifically for conditions involving agitation and elevated blood pressure.

modern_research.summary: Reserpine isolated 1952. Became the first effective antihypertensive AND first antipsychotic drug. Directly enabled the psychiatric revolution of the 1950s.
modern_research.significance: Arguably the single most consequential traditional medicine discovery of the 20th century. 3,000 years of Ayurvedic use mapped precisely onto reserpine's mechanism.

plants.evidence_authority: Reserpine — FDA approved antihypertensive (still in USP)

ayurveda.translation: Haridra (turmeric) — reduces pitta, improves complexion, heals wounds. Applied to wounds to prevent infection. Taken internally for liver complaints and digestive disorders.
ayurveda.preparation: Powder in warm milk; paste applied to wounds
ayurveda.condition: Inflammation, wound infection, liver disease, skin conditions

chinese.original: 姜黄 破血，行氣，通經，止痛
chinese.translation: Jiānghuáng — breaks up blood stagnation, moves qi, opens meridians, stops pain. Used for chest and abdominal pain from blood stagnation and swellings from trauma.
chinese.preparation: Root decocted in water; powdered and applied topically
chinese.condition: Pain from trauma, menstrual irregularity, inflammatory swellings

modern_research.summary: Over 3,000 published studies. Strong anti-inflammatory activity. Bioavailability limitations complicate pharmaceutical development but topical applications show strong results.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Independently documented by Ayurvedic and Chinese traditions for overlapping inflammatory conditions.

plants.evidence_authority: Curcumin — Cochrane review: anti-inflammatory evidence; not FDA approved as drug
plants.evidence_note: GRAS status in food; IND studies ongoing
plants.priority_reason: 8 independent traditions agree · Cochrane reviewed but no drug approval · condition affects hundreds of millions

chinese.source_text: Zhouhou Beiji Fang (Ge Hong, c.340 CE)
chinese.original: 青蒿一握，以水二升漬，絞取汁，盡服之。（治寒熱諸瘧）
chinese.translation: Take a handful of qinghao, soak in two sheng of water, wring out the juice, and drink it all. For the treatment of intermittent cold and heat fevers of all types — malaria.
chinese.preparation: Whole plant cold-soaked in water and juice extracted (NOT boiled — heat destroys artemisinin)
chinese.notes: This is the exact passage Tu Youyou read in 1971 that led her to artemisinin. The cold-water method Ge Hong specified preserved artemisinin's activity — the critical methodological clue.

modern_research.summary: Artemisinin isolated 1972. WHO essential medicine. Most effective antimalarial drug. Saves approximately 1 million lives annually. Nobel Prize 2015 to Tu Youyou.
modern_research.significance: The defining proof-of-concept for this database's thesis. A 4th century Chinese text contained the key to the world's most important antimalarial drug.

plants.evidence_authority: Artemisinin/Artesunate — WHO EML 23rd list (2023); Nobel Prize 2015

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides (77 CE)
greek_roman.original: ὁ τοῦ ἡμέρου μήκωνος ὀπὸς ναρκωτικός ἐστι καὶ ἀνώδυνος καὶ ὑπνωτικός
greek_roman.translation: The juice of the cultivated poppy is narcotic, pain-relieving, and sleep-inducing. A small amount relieves pain and promotes sleep; it calms coughs and eases diarrhea. Too large a dose causes a sleep from which there is no waking.
greek_roman.preparation: Dried latex (opium) dissolved in wine or water; small dose taken orally
greek_roman.condition: Pain, insomnia, cough, diarrhea
greek_roman.notes: Dioscorides' precise dosage warning shows sophisticated understanding of the lethal dose threshold.

egyptian.original: [Hieratic] spnn — remedy for crying children: poppy seed prepared and given for four days
egyptian.translation: Remedy for excessive crying in children: poppy seeds prepared as a soothing drink administered for four days.
egyptian.notes: Earliest documented pharmacological use of opium — approximately 1550 BCE.

unani.source_text: Canon of Medicine, Ibn Sina (c.1025 CE)
unani.original: الأفيون يسكّن الألم ويُنيم، ولكنه في كثيره مُهلك
unani.translation: Opium calms pain and induces sleep, but in large amounts it is lethal. The physician must exercise great caution with the dose.
unani.preparation: Small amounts in wine or water

modern_research.summary: Morphine isolated 1804 — the birth of pharmaceutical chemistry. Entire opioid pharmacology derives from Papaver somniferum.
modern_research.significance: MAXIMUM CROSS-TRADITION CONVERGENCE: Egyptian (1550 BCE), Greek (77 CE), and Islamic (1025 CE) independently document identical therapeutic uses and identical dosage warnings.

plants.evidence_authority: Morphine — WHO EML core list; codeine WHO EML; papaverine WHO EML

greek_roman.source_text: Hippocratic Corpus (c.400 BCE) / De Materia Medica, Dioscorides
greek_roman.original: ἡ ἰτέα — τὸ φύλλον λεῖον μεθ᾽ ὕδατος πινόμενον κοιλίας στρόφους παύει
greek_roman.translation: The willow — its leaf ground and drunk with water stops belly pains. The bark dried and ground in wine cures fever. Hippocrates prescribed willow leaf tea for fever and pain in women during childbirth.
greek_roman.preparation: Bark or leaves decocted in water or wine
greek_roman.condition: Fever, pain, inflammation

egyptian.original: [Hieratic] t3y-n-itrw — remedy for heat in the limbs and inflammation
egyptian.translation: Willow leaves — remedy for heat in the limbs and for inflammation. Applied as a poultice to swollen areas; decocted for systemic fever.
egyptian.preparation: Leaves as poultice; bark decocted internally

north_american_indigenous.source_text: Moerman's Native American Ethnobotany Database — Cherokee, Ojibwe, Iroquois and others
north_american_indigenous.translation: Willow bark decocted and drunk for fever, headache, and pain. Chewed for toothache. Applied as poultice for swelling and sore joints. Documented independently across nations.
north_american_indigenous.preparation: Inner bark decocted; leaves as poultice

chinese.original: 柳树皮 — 去风，消肿，解热
chinese.translation: Willow bark — dispels wind, reduces swelling, resolves fever. Used for rheumatic joint pain, edema, and heat diseases.

modern_research.summary: Salicin isolated 1828. Aspirin synthesized 1897. Most widely used drug in history. Anti-inflammatory mechanism discovered by John Vane, Nobel Prize 1982.
modern_research.significance: MAXIMUM GLOBAL CONVERGENCE: Egyptian (1550 BCE), Greek (400 BCE), Chinese, and Native American (multiple independent nations) all independently discovered willow for fever and pain.

plants.evidence_authority: Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) — FDA approved 1899; most widely used drug in history

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides
greek_roman.original: κολχικόν — ῥίζα πινομένη στρόφους κοιλίας λύει
greek_roman.translation: Colchicum — the root taken in drink relieves gripping pains in the belly. Used for the painful swellings of the joints that afflict those who eat richly.
greek_roman.preparation: Root dried and powdered, taken in small amounts
greek_roman.condition: Gout (podagra), joint inflammation
greek_roman.notes: The recognition of narrow therapeutic window mirrors modern understanding of colchicine's therapeutic index.

unani.original: سرنجان — ينفع النقرس والمفاصل المتورمة إذا أخذ بمقدار
unani.translation: Suranjan — beneficial for gout (niqris) and swollen joints when taken in the right measure. Dangerous in excess. Most effective for the hot, swollen joint pain that comes with rich diet.
unani.preparation: Root powder in small precise doses
unani.notes: Ibn Sina connects rich-diet lifestyle to gout — accurate understanding of hyperuricemia's dietary link, 900 years before uric acid was identified.

modern_research.summary: Colchicine isolated 1820. Still first-line treatment for acute gout attacks. Also used for familial Mediterranean fever and pericarditis.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Greek and Islamic traditions independently document the same plant for gout with the same dosage caution. Colchicine remains gold-standard treatment.

plants.evidence_authority: Colchicine — FDA approved for gout and familial Mediterranean fever (Colcrys, Gloperba)

tradition_names.west_african: Esère / Ordeal bean (Efik people, Calabar, Nigeria)

west_african.source_text: Documented by W.F. Daniel (1840s); Efik oral tradition, Calabar region, Nigeria
west_african.translation: The esère bean is used in the ordeal of the accused — the person drinks a decoction. If innocent, they vomit quickly and survive; if guilty, they die. The bean is also used by healers to treat eye ailments that cause clouding of vision.
west_african.preparation: Beans ground and decocted; weaker preparations for eye conditions
west_african.condition: Judicial ordeal; glaucoma (eye clouding); ritual use
west_african.notes: The ordeal is pharmacologically explainable: strong dose causes lethal paralysis; smaller dose causes immediate vomiting (survival). The eye clouding use maps precisely onto glaucoma.

modern_research.summary: Physostigmine isolated 1863. First effective glaucoma treatment. Mechanism: acetylcholinesterase inhibition — foundational discovery for understanding neurotransmission. Led to nerve agent antidotes, Alzheimer's treatments, and modern neuropharmacology.
modern_research.significance: West African healers identified glaucoma and found a pharmacological treatment. Acetylcholinesterase inhibition discovered through physostigmine enabled entire field of cholinergic pharmacology.

plants.evidence_authority: Physostigmine — FDA approved for anticholinergic toxicity reversal; neostigmine FDA approved

tradition_names.amazonian: Ourari / Woorali (multiple nations — Yanomami, Makushi, Wapishana)

amazonian.source_text: Documented by Schomburgk (1835), Waterton (1812); multiple Amazonian nations
amazonian.translation: The ourari is made by boiling the bark and roots of the woorali vine with other plants over many days, reducing to a thick paste. Applied to arrow tips, it causes the struck target to lose all muscular movement while remaining conscious. Death comes from inability to breathe. The preparation requires specific knowledge of which plants to combine.
amazonian.preparation: Bark and roots of multiple species boiled and reduced to paste; applied to arrow tips
amazonian.condition: Hunting paralysis (arrow poison); traditional healers used weaker preparations for muscle spasm
amazonian.notes: The knowledge that certain vine combinations produce selective muscle paralysis — leaving consciousness intact — is a pharmacological observation of extraordinary precision.

modern_research.summary: Tubocurarine isolated 1935. Introduced into surgery 1942. Enabled modern surgery by providing controlled reversible muscle relaxation. All subsequent neuromuscular blocking agents are synthetic descendants.
modern_research.significance: Without curare-derived compounds, modern surgical procedures as currently practiced would be impossible. Amazonian hunters were applying, empirically, the pharmacological principle enabling modern surgery.

plants.evidence_authority: Tubocurarine — FDA approved neuromuscular blocker; all modern surgical relaxants are synthetic descendants

tradition_names.amazonian: Ayahuasca / Yagé / Caapi (Shipibo, Shuar, and others)

amazonian.source_text: Documented by Spruce (1851), Schultes (20th century); Shipibo, Shuar, and other nations
amazonian.original: [Multiple Amazonian languages — Quechua: aya = spirit, waska = vine]
amazonian.translation: The ayahuasca is prepared by boiling the caapi vine with chacruna leaves over many hours. Alone, neither plant produces the vision — only together do they open the sight. Used for healing of illness of the spirit and body that ordinary medicine cannot reach — for sorrow that does not end, for trauma, for seeing the true cause of disease.
amazonian.preparation: B. caapi vine bark boiled with P. viridis leaves for hours; combination is essential — neither alone is orally active
amazonian.notes: P. viridis contains DMT (orally inactive alone). B. caapi contains beta-carboline MAO inhibitors allowing oral DMT activity. The probability of discovering this two-plant combination empirically is extremely low — implies systematic experimentation.

modern_research.summary: Multiple Phase 2/3 trials showing remarkable efficacy for treatment-resistant depression and PTSD. FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation under review. The ceremonial context indigenous practice systematized may be therapeutically essential — modern trials finding the same.
modern_research.significance: The ayahuasca combination is the most pharmacologically sophisticated traditional discovery in this database — two plants with specific complementary mechanisms, combined deliberately for an effect neither produces alone.

plants.evidence_authority: DMT/Ayahuasca — FDA Breakthrough Therapy (psilocybin, related); ayahuasca Phase 2 trials at Imperial College, Johns Hopkins
plants.priority_reason: FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation · 6 traditions · active Phase 2/3 trials

tradition_names.north_american_indigenous: Icháⁿ hú (Lakota) — multiple names across nations

north_american_indigenous.source_text: Moerman's Native American Ethnobotany Database — Lakota, Cheyenne, Comanche, Kiowa, Pawnee, Omaha and others (14 distinct nations)
north_american_indigenous.translation: The root of the purple coneflower is chewed for toothache, sore throat, and infection. Decocted for cough and fever. The juice applied to wounds to prevent infection. Used for snakebite — root chewed and applied to bite and given as drink. Documented independently across 14 plains nations for similar purposes.
north_american_indigenous.preparation: Root chewed raw; decocted for systemic use; juice applied topically
north_american_indigenous.condition: Infection, wound healing, snakebite, toothache, immune support
north_american_indigenous.notes: Documented across 14 independent nations for overlapping uses — strong internal convergence within North American traditions.

modern_research.summary: Cochrane review found modest evidence for reducing duration of common cold. Immune stimulation effects biologically plausible. One of the most commercially successful herbal medicines in the West.
modern_research.significance: Cross-nation convergence within Native American traditions (14 documented nations) is itself a strong validation signal. Modern research confirms meaningful but modest immune modulation.

plants.evidence_authority: Echinacea — Cochrane review 2015: modest reduction in cold duration/severity confirmed
plants.priority_reason: 7 independent traditions agree · Cochrane reviewed but no drug approval · condition affects hundreds of millions

egyptian.original: [Hieratic] bit — wound dressing: honey applied to wound, bound with linen
egyptian.translation: Honey used extensively for wound dressings. For infected wounds: apply honey thickly to the wound, cover with linen. Appears in 500 of the 877 prescriptions in the Ebers Papyrus.
egyptian.preparation: Applied directly to wounds; diluted for oral and eye use
egyptian.condition: Wound infection, eye infection, digestive complaints

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides
greek_roman.original: τὸ μέλι — ἕλκη κεχρονισμένα καθαίρει καὶ ἀνακαθαίρει
greek_roman.translation: Honey — cleanses and clears chronic ulcers. Boiled and applied to festering wounds it draws out the corruption. Mixed with vinegar given for coughs.
greek_roman.preparation: Applied raw or boiled to wounds; mixed with vinegar for cough
greek_roman.condition: Wound infection, cough, chronic ulcers

ayurveda.translation: Madhu (honey) — cleanses wounds, treats eye diseases, relieves cough, promotes healing. Applied to wounds it dries them and prevents putrefaction.
ayurveda.preparation: Applied topically to wounds; taken with herbs for respiratory conditions

modern_research.summary: Multiple genuine antimicrobial mechanisms simultaneously. Manuka honey licensed as medical-grade wound dressing in UK. Active against MRSA.
modern_research.significance: MAXIMUM GLOBAL CONVERGENCE: Egyptian (1550 BCE), Greek (400 BCE), Ayurvedic, Islamic, and Native American traditions all independently discovered honey's wound-healing and antimicrobial properties.

plants.evidence_authority: Medical-grade honey (Medihoney) — FDA 510(k) cleared wound care device; methylglyoxal activity documented
plants.evidence_note: WHO recognizes honey in wound care guidelines

kampo.source_text: Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) / Yoshimasu Todo, Yakucho
kampo.original: 麻黄 — 発汗、利水、平喘。寒邪による喘息・浮腫・発熱に用いる。
kampo.translation: Maōu — induces sweating, promotes urination, calms wheezing. Used for asthma, edema, and fever caused by cold invasion. Kampo dosing refined over centuries: smaller doses than Chinese tradition, reflecting Japanese clinical observation of dose-dependent side effects.
kampo.preparation: Decocted in water; standard Kampo formula dosing 3–9g dried herb
kampo.condition: Asthma, bronchitis, nasal congestion, edema, fever with absence of sweating
kampo.notes: Kampo standardized dosing protocols for ma huang centuries before Western pharmacology isolated ephedrine. The Kampo clinical tradition's caution about cardiac effects in high doses anticipates modern contraindications.

chinese.source_text: Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草經, c.200 CE)
chinese.original: 麻黄，味苦，温。主中風傷寒頭痛，温瘧，發表出汗，去邪熱氣，止咳逆上氣，除寒熱，破癥堅積聚。
chinese.translation: Ma huang, bitter in taste, warm in nature. Indicated for wind-cold with headache, malarial fever, promotes sweating to expel pathogenic factors, clears fever-heat, stops coughing and wheezing, dispels cold-heat, breaks up accumulations.
chinese.preparation: Decocted in water; often combined with cinnamon twig (guizhi) in classical formulas

modern_research.summary: Ephedrine isolated 1885 by Nagai Nagayoshi (Japanese chemist). Pseudoephedrine still the primary ingredient in most OTC decongestants worldwide. Ephedrine used in anesthesia for hypotension management. Banned as dietary supplement in many countries due to cardiac risk at high doses — validating Kampo's centuries-old dosing caution.
modern_research.significance: The Kampo tradition's empirical dose refinement — using smaller amounts than classical Chinese medicine — was pharmacologically correct: ephedrine's therapeutic window is narrow and cardiac risk is dose-dependent. Nagai, a Japanese chemist trained in both Western and Kampo traditions, isolated ephedrine specifically because of its documented clinical use.

plants.evidence_authority: Ephedrine — FDA approved vasopressor; pseudoephedrine FDA approved decongestant
plants.evidence_note: Ephedra supplements banned but isolated alkaloids approved

kampo.source_text: Honzo Wamyo (918 CE); used in ~70% of classical Kampo formulas
kampo.original: 甘草 — 甘、平。補脾益氣、清熱解毒、祛痰止咳、緩急止痛、調和諸薬。
kampo.translation: Kanzō — sweet in taste, neutral. Tonifies the spleen, clears heat and toxins, eliminates phlegm and stops cough, relieves cramping pain, and harmonises the actions of other herbs. Used as a modulator in compound formulas — the most frequently prescribed single herb in all of Kampo.
kampo.preparation: Decocted with other herbs; used in approximately 70% of all classical Kampo formulas as harmonising agent
kampo.condition: Cough, inflammation, peptic ulcer, adrenal insufficiency, drug toxicity modulation
kampo.notes: Kampo's use of licorice as a formula harmoniser — reducing side effects of other herbs and improving palatability — is now understood pharmacologically: glycyrrhizin modulates cortisol metabolism and has genuine anti-inflammatory activity that may reduce the adverse effects of other botanical compounds in a formula.

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides (77 CE)
greek_roman.original: γλυκύρριζα — ἡ ῥίζα χρησιμεύει πρὸς βῆχας καὶ ξηρασίαν στόματος
greek_roman.translation: Glykyrrhiza — the root is useful for coughs and dryness of the mouth. Dissolved in rainwater it relieves coughs and all chest and lung conditions.
greek_roman.preparation: Root dissolved in water; taken orally
greek_roman.condition: Cough, chest conditions, mouth dryness

modern_research.summary: Glycyrrhizin has documented anti-inflammatory, antiviral (including SARS-CoV inhibition), and hepatoprotective activity. Carbenoxolone (synthetic derivative) was a major peptic ulcer treatment before PPIs. At high doses, glycyrrhizin causes pseudoaldosteronism (hypertension, hypokalemia) — validating traditional dose caution.
modern_research.significance: MAXIMUM GLOBAL CONVERGENCE: Egyptian (Ebers Papyrus), Greek (Dioscorides), Chinese, Islamic, and Kampo traditions independently documented licorice root for cough and inflammation. Among the most globally widespread traditional medicine discoveries. Modern research validates the anti-inflammatory and antiviral activity but also confirms the dose-dependent adverse effects that multiple traditions cautioned against.

plants.evidence_authority: Glycyrrhizin — Cochrane review on licorice for various conditions; not FDA approved as drug but GRAS
plants.priority_reason: 10 independent traditions agree · Cochrane reviewed but no drug approval · condition affects hundreds of millions

tibetan.source_text: Gyushi (rGyud bzhi), Chapter on mountain medicine plants; Men-Tsee-Khang formulary
tibetan.translation: Somaraja — a mountain-born medicine. Increases physical strength of the body, clarifies the mind. Used for exhaustion at high altitude, for those weakened by thin air, and to sustain the body during long journeys over mountain passes.
tibetan.preparation: Root dried and decocted; often combined with other altitude-medicine plants in Gyushi formulas
tibetan.condition: Altitude sickness, fatigue, mental fog, physical exhaustion
tibetan.notes: Rhodiola's documented use in Tibetan medicine specifically for altitude-related fatigue and cognitive impairment is a remarkable pre-scientific identification of what modern research now calls adaptogenic activity. The Gyushi was written at 3,500–4,500m altitude — Tibetan physicians had more clinical experience with altitude physiology than any other medical tradition on Earth.

chinese.source_text: Bencao Gangmu Shiyi (supplement, 18th c.); modern Tibetan-Chinese materia medica
chinese.original: 红景天 — 益气清肺，止咳平喘。用于气虚体弱，气短乏力。
chinese.translation: Hóng jǐng tiān — tonifies qi and clears the lungs, stops cough and calms wheezing. Used for qi deficiency with physical weakness, shortness of breath and fatigue.
chinese.preparation: Root decocted or taken as powder

modern_research.summary: Multiple clinical trials showing Rhodiola reduces mental and physical fatigue, improves altitude tolerance, and has mild antidepressant effects. Swedish Space Corporation used Rhodiola in cosmonauts. US military-funded research for altitude performance. Some RCT evidence for burnout syndrome reduction.
modern_research.significance: Tibetan medicine's specific identification of Rhodiola for altitude fatigue and cognitive impairment maps precisely onto modern adaptogen research. The tradition's 1,200+ years of clinical observation at actual altitude constitutes a natural longitudinal study no modern trial can replicate.

plants.evidence_authority: Salidroside/Rosavin — Cochrane review on adaptogens; not FDA approved as drug
plants.priority_reason: 7 independent traditions agree · Cochrane reviewed but no drug approval · condition affects hundreds of millions

maya.source_text: Ritual of the Bacabs / Yucatec Maya oral tradition; documented by Roys (1965) and modern ethnobotanists
maya.original: [Yucatec Maya oral tradition — transcribed] Chay — ku yantal u pixan patan. Ku ts'áabal ti' máako'ob yéetel k'áak'. Yaan u páajtal u ts'o'okol u yik'nal.
maya.translation: Chay — it sustains the vital spirit of the body. It is given to people with fever. It is capable of cooling the heat within. Used by Maya healers for generations to strengthen blood, support the heart, and treat digestive ailments. The leaves must be cooked before eating — raw leaves contain toxic compounds that cooking deactivates.
maya.preparation: Leaves must be boiled for 5+ minutes to deactivate hydrogen cyanide precursors; then eaten as vegetable or decocted as tea
maya.condition: Malnutrition, fever, digestive complaints, blood building
maya.notes: The Maya knowledge that chaya leaves must be cooked before consumption — and that raw leaves are harmful — represents precise pharmacological understanding of hydrolysable cyanogenic glycosides without any knowledge of chemistry. This preparation knowledge is itself a sophisticated folk pharmacological insight predating any understanding of the underlying mechanism.

modern_research.key_compound: Isothiocyanates, vitamins A/C/K, iron, calcium; cyanogenic glycosides (deactivated by cooking)
modern_research.summary: Chaya has exceptional nutritional density — higher protein, vitamin C, calcium, and iron than most commonly consumed vegetables. Studies show hypoglycemic effects in diabetic animal models and some human pilot studies. The raw toxicity / cooked safety distinction is fully validated: raw leaves contain linamarin which releases HCN; cooking for 5+ minutes deactivates it.
modern_research.significance: The Maya preparation protocol for chaya — cook to neutralise toxins — is precise ethnopharmacological knowledge that modern chemistry confirms completely. The anti-diabetic potential is particularly noteworthy given that modern Mexican and Central American populations have very high diabetes rates and chaya remains a staple traditional food.

plants.evidence_authority: Isothiocyanates — research stage; no specific Cochrane or FDA approval for Chaya
plants.priority_reason: 3 traditions agree · moderate disease burden · active compounds identified but uninvestigated clinically

philippine.source_text: Quisumbing, Medicinal Plants of the Philippines (1951); Philippine DOH Traditional Medicine Program
philippine.original: [Tagalog oral tradition, documented] Ang lagundi ay ginagamit sa ubo, lagnat, at sakit ng katawan. Ang dahon ay niluluto sa tubig at inumin para sa hika at sipon.
philippine.translation: Lagundi is used for cough, fever, and body pain. The leaves are boiled in water and the decoction drunk for asthma and colds. Applied as a poultice to painful joints. One of the most widely used medicinal plants across all Philippine ethnic groups — used by Tagalog, Visayan, Ilocano, and indigenous groups alike, suggesting independent convergent discovery within the Philippine archipelago.
philippine.preparation: Leaves boiled in water, drunk as tea for respiratory conditions; leaf poultice for pain
philippine.condition: Cough, asthma, bronchitis, fever, joint pain, headache
philippine.notes: Lagundi is one of only 10 herbal medicines formally approved by the Philippine Department of Health (DOH) as an official alternative medicine — backed by clinical trials required for DOH registration. This makes it one of the best-validated traditional medicines globally in terms of government-mandated clinical evidence.

modern_research.summary: DOH-approved for cough and asthma. Multiple Philippine clinical trials showing bronchodilator and antitussive activity. Casticin confirmed as anti-inflammatory compound. Anti-histamine activity demonstrated in in vitro studies. Now commercially available as standardised tablet formulation (Ascof) in Philippine pharmacies.
modern_research.significance: One of the most completely validated traditional-to-pharmaceutical transitions in Southeast Asian medicine — government-mandated clinical trials, standardised formulation, commercial availability, and continued use by practitioners at all levels of the Philippine healthcare system simultaneously.

plants.evidence_authority: Lagundi — FDA IND studies; Philippine FDA approved; WHO included in traditional medicine guidelines

plants.common_name: Garlic + Bile + Copper Vessel (Bald's Eye Remedy)

tradition_names.celtic_norse: Læcedóm wið eagena sáre (Remedy for eye disease) — Anglo-Saxon, England

plants.modern_botanical: Allium sativum + Allium cepa (with Brassica species) + copper vessel

celtic_norse.source_text: Bald's Leechbook (Medicinale Anglicum), c.900–950 CE, British Library Royal MS 12 D XVII
celtic_norse.original: Wið eagena sár: genim cropleac ond gárleác, begea emfela, gecnua wel tosomne, nim þonne win ond gallán fearres, begea emfela, meng wið þy leáccræfte, do þonne on ærene fæt, læt standan nigun niht on þam ærene fæte.
celtic_norse.translation: For soreness of the eyes: take cropleac [onion or leek] and garlic, of both equal amounts, pound well together, then take wine and bull's gall, of both equal amounts, mix with the plant preparation, then put into a brass [copper] vessel, let stand nine nights in the brass vessel. Wring through a cloth, clarify well, put into a horn. About night time apply with a feather to the eye.
celtic_norse.preparation: Garlic and onion/leek pounded together; mixed with wine and ox bile; stored in copper vessel 9 days; strained and applied to eye with feather
celtic_norse.condition: Eye infection (stye, conjunctivitis); potentially MRSA and other bacterial infections
celtic_norse.notes: This recipe was tested by microbiologists at the University of Nottingham (Freya Harrison et al., 2015) as part of the AncientBiotics project. Results: the preparation killed approximately 90% of MRSA bacteria in laboratory conditions — significantly outperforming modern antibiotics against some MRSA strains. Each ingredient alone had minimal effect; the combination and copper vessel were essential. Published in Scientific Reports. The copper vessel acts as an oligodynamic antimicrobial agent — the same mechanism independently discovered by Egyptian medicine.

modern_research.key_compound: Allicin (garlic) + taurocholic acid (bile) + copper ions (vessel) — synergistic combination
modern_research.summary: Harrison et al. (2015, Scientific Reports) demonstrated 90% MRSA kill rate. Each ingredient tested separately showed minimal activity; the full combination in copper vessel was required. Active against Staphylococcus aureus biofilms — particularly relevant as biofilm-associated MRSA is resistant to most modern antibiotics. Mechanism: copper ions + allicin disrupt bacterial membrane; bile acids increase membrane permeability; combined action penetrates biofilm.
modern_research.significance: One of the most dramatic ethnopharmacological validations of the 21st century: a 1,100-year-old Anglo-Saxon recipe for eye infections was tested against MRSA — a 21st century superbug that didn't exist in 900 CE — and outperformed many modern antibiotics. The copper vessel detail (often dismissed by earlier scholars as ritual/symbolic) proved pharmacologically essential, validating Bald's Leechbook as a genuine empirical medical text rather than superstition. The same copper oligodynamic mechanism was independently discovered by Egyptian medicine ~2,500 years earlier.

plants.evidence_authority: Allicin — Cochrane review on garlic for cardiovascular; Bald's eye remedy validated UCL 2015
plants.priority_reason: 6 independent traditions agree · Cochrane reviewed but no drug approval · condition affects hundreds of millions

persian_zoroastrian.source_text: Bundahishn (9th c. CE); Avestan medical references; Persian traditional use
persian_zoroastrian.original: زرپران — از گیاهان مقدس ایران باستان. برای غم و اندوه، برای تقویت قلب، و برای بیماری‌های زنان به کار می‌رفت.
persian_zoroastrian.translation: Zarparan — among the sacred plants of ancient Iran. Used for grief and sorrow, for strengthening the heart, and for women's diseases. The Zoroastrian texts associate saffron with the sacred fire and with the purification of the spirit. Given to those afflicted by dark moods and unquiet hearts.
persian_zoroastrian.preparation: Stigmas steeped in warm water or milk; taken orally
persian_zoroastrian.condition: Depression, grief, heart weakness, menstrual disorders
persian_zoroastrian.notes: Pre-Islamic Persian medicine's use of saffron specifically for 'dark moods and unquiet hearts' aligns with extraordinary precision with modern RCT findings — multiple trials showing saffron comparable to fluoxetine for mild-to-moderate depression.

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides (77 CE)
greek_roman.original: κρόκος — ἀλγήματα τῆς κεφαλῆς παύει, ὑπνωτικός ἐστι
greek_roman.translation: Krokos — relieves pains of the head, is sleep-inducing. Applied with honey and wine it is good for inflammation. Taken as a drink it sharpens the senses and lifts the spirits.
greek_roman.preparation: Steeped in wine or water; applied topically with honey
greek_roman.condition: Headache, insomnia, inflammation, low spirits

modern_research.summary: Multiple RCTs (including a 2013 meta-analysis in Human Psychopharmacology) showing saffron extract comparable to fluoxetine (Prozac) and imipramine for mild-to-moderate depression. Mechanism: serotonin reuptake inhibition and monoamine activity. Also shows promise for Alzheimer's (improving cognitive scores in trials). Growing body of evidence for cardiovascular protection via crocin's antioxidant activity.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Persian Zoroastrian, Islamic, Ayurvedic, and Greek/Roman traditions all independently document saffron for depression/low spirits. Modern RCTs validate the antidepressant effect with a mechanism (serotonin reuptake) that makes the convergent folk observation pharmacologically comprehensible. With multiple RCTs now published, saffron represents one of the best-evidenced traditional antidepressants in the world — almost entirely unknown to mainstream psychiatry.

plants.evidence_authority: Crocin/Safranal — Cochrane review on saffron for depression 2019; multiple RCTs
plants.priority_reason: 9 independent traditions agree · Cochrane reviewed but no drug approval · condition affects hundreds of millions

tradition_names.australian_indigenous: Bundjalung: healing leaves (name varies by language group)

australian_indigenous.source_text: Published ethnobotanical literature with documented Bundjalung community involvement; Lassak & McCarthy (1983); Brophy et al. (CSIRO)
australian_indigenous.original: [Bundjalung oral tradition, documented with community consent] The leaves of the paperbark tree were crushed and inhaled for coughs and colds. Crushed leaves were applied to cuts, burns, and infected skin. The swamps where Melaleuca grew were known as healing places — people bathed in the water to treat skin conditions.
australian_indigenous.translation: Tea tree leaves crushed and inhaled for respiratory conditions. Leaf poultice applied to wounds, cuts, burns, and skin infections. Bathing in natural Melaleuca swamps for skin disease. This use was documented by the Bundjalung people of northern New South Wales with their involvement in the research process.
australian_indigenous.preparation: Leaves crushed for inhalation; poultice of crushed leaves applied to skin; bathing in swamps
australian_indigenous.condition: Wound infection, skin disease, respiratory conditions
australian_indigenous.notes: ⚠️ This entry documents knowledge already in the published academic record with documented community involvement. The Bundjalung people's use of tea tree was documented by ethnobotanists in collaboration with the community. The commercialisation of tea tree oil is a case study in both successful traditional-to-pharmaceutical transfer and the complex intellectual property questions around indigenous knowledge — the Bundjalung community received no compensation for the global tea tree oil industry.

modern_research.summary: Tea tree oil is extensively validated as a topical antimicrobial — effective against S. aureus (including some MRSA strains), Candida, herpes simplex, and numerous other pathogens. European Pharmacopoeia standard exists. Used in licensed wound care products, acne preparations, and nail fungal treatments globally.
modern_research.significance: Represents both the extraordinary pharmacological richness of Aboriginal plant knowledge and the complex ethics of indigenous intellectual property: a traditional remedy now constituting a global industry worth hundreds of millions of dollars annually, with essentially no benefit flowing to the Bundjalung community who identified and used the plant for millennia.

plants.evidence_authority: Terpinen-4-ol — FDA approved in OTC antiseptic products; TGA (Australia) approved

kampo.source_text: Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE); Daio-kanzoto formula in Ishinho (984 CE)
kampo.original: 大黄、和名 加波良久佐。性寒、味苦。主下瘀血、血閉、蕩滌腸胃、推陳致新。
kampo.translation: Daio (rhubarb), Japanese name Kawarakusa. Nature: cold. Taste: bitter. Primary uses: dispels static blood, opens blocked blood, flushes and purges the intestines and stomach, eliminates the old and generates the new. Kampo preparation: shorter cooking time than Chinese tradition preserves cathartic anthraquinones; longer cooking destroys them, producing only astringent/anti-inflammatory effect. This cooking-time distinction was documented empirically in Kampo centuries before the active compounds were identified.
kampo.preparation: Root decocted 3–12g; cooking time determines effect — short = laxative, long = anti-inflammatory
kampo.condition: Constipation, intestinal inflammation, blood stasis conditions
kampo.notes: Kampo's empirical discovery that cooking time changes rhubarb's clinical effect (short cooking = cathartic, long cooking = astringent) is a precise pharmacological observation: anthraquinone glycosides (cathartic) are heat-labile; tannins (astringent) are heat-stable. This distinction was not understood chemically until the 19th century.

modern_research.key_compound: Anthraquinone glycosides (sennoside A, B), tannins, stilbenes (rhapontigenin)
modern_research.summary: Rhubarb root is official in multiple modern pharmacopeias. Sennosides A and B are active cathartic compounds also found in senna — both now pharmaceutical. Rhubarb compounds show hepatoprotective, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory activity in research. Used in Japanese intensive care for preventing bacterial translocation in ICU patients.
modern_research.significance: Kampo's cooking-time-dependent effect distinction — cathartic if undercooked, astringent if fully cooked — is one of the most precise empirical pharmacological observations in traditional medicine, predating understanding of anthraquinone heat lability by centuries.

plants.evidence_authority: Sennosides — FDA approved OTC laxative (senna/rhubarb); in USP

kampo.source_text: Honzo Wamyo (918 CE); Sho-saiko-to formula, Ishinho (984 CE)
kampo.original: 柴胡、和名 美之末。苦、平。主心腹腸胃中結気、寒熱邪気、推陳致新。
kampo.translation: Saikou (Bupleurum), Japanese name Mishima. Bitter, neutral. Main: constricted qi in heart/abdomen/intestines, alternating cold and heat from pathogens, eliminates old and generates new. Sho-saiko-to (Minor Bupleurum Decoction): the most extensively studied Kampo formula for hepatitis. Multiple large Japanese RCTs demonstrated reduction in liver inflammation markers, HBsAg levels, and liver cancer risk in cirrhotic patients. Contraindication discovered 1994: combined with interferon causes severe interstitial pneumonia — a post-marketing pharmacovigilance discovery that became a model for herb-drug interaction monitoring.
kampo.preparation: Root decocted 5–8g; Sho-saiko-to combines with 6 other herbs
kampo.condition: Chronic hepatitis, liver inflammation, alternating fever/chills, depression
kampo.notes: The 1994 Sho-saiko-to / interferon interaction discovery is the most important herb-drug interaction case in Kampo pharmacovigilance history — it led to mandatory warning labels and a model for monitoring traditional medicine drug interactions in Japan's national health system.

modern_research.key_compound: Saikosaponins (a, b1, b2, c, d), bupleurumol
modern_research.summary: Saikosaponins have documented anti-inflammatory activity via glucocorticoid receptor modulation, hepatoprotective effects, and antiviral activity against HBV. Sho-saiko-to: multiple Japanese RCTs for chronic hepatitis B/C showing significant reduction in liver fibrosis markers. Also emerging research for depression via HPA axis modulation.
modern_research.significance: Represents the most rigorously studied Kampo formula with both positive clinical evidence (hepatoprotection) and a critical safety signal (interferon interaction) — making it a model for how traditional medicine evidence should be evaluated: both efficacy and safety, with pharmacovigilance taken seriously.

plants.evidence_authority: Saikosaponins — Cochrane/systematic reviews on Sho-saiko-to for hepatitis
plants.priority_reason: 7 independent traditions agree · Cochrane reviewed but no drug approval · condition affects hundreds of millions

tibetan.source_text: Gyushi (rGyud bzhi), Chapter on mountain medicines; Men-Tsee-Khang formulary
tibetan.translation: Arura — the king of medicines. Beneficial for all diseases. Called the medicine of gods and demons. Possesses all five tastes (sour, sweet, bitter, pungent, astringent — only salt absent). Therefore can treat all three humors. Long-term use extends life and sharpens the mind. The Three Fruits formula (Arura + Baru + Kyuru) is the foundation of Tibetan pharmacology.
tibetan.preparation: Fruit dried and powdered; taken with different vehicles depending on condition — warm water, honey, molasses, or butter
tibetan.condition: All diseases (general tonic); specifically digestive disorders, respiratory conditions, wound healing

ayurveda.translation: Haritaki — destroyer of all diseases. One of the most important herbs in Ayurveda; used in Triphala formula (same Three Fruits concept as Tibetan medicine). Specifically valuable for eye diseases, digestive disorders, and as a rejuvenating rasayana.
ayurveda.preparation: Fruit powdered; taken as Triphala with warm water before bed
ayurveda.condition: Digestive disorders, eye disease, constipation, aging

modern_research.key_compound: Chebulagic acid, chebulinic acid, corilagin, gallic acid, ellagic acid
modern_research.summary: Chebulagic acid and chebulinic acid show documented antiviral activity against HSV-1, HSV-2, and HIV integrase inhibition in vitro. Antimicrobial against MRSA. Strong antioxidant. Triphala (three myrobalans) has clinical evidence for constipation, weight loss, and dental health (mouthwash trials).
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Tibetan and Ayurvedic traditions independently developed the same Three Fruits formula (Triphala / Arura-Baru-Kyuru) using the same primary ingredient — Terminalia chebula — for overlapping indications. This convergence between two neighboring high-altitude civilizations is strong, and connects further to Australian Indigenous use of Terminalia ferdinandiana — three independent traditions identifying the Terminalia genus as uniquely medicinally important.

plants.evidence_authority: Chebulagic acid — Cochrane on Triphala for constipation; dental health RCTs
plants.priority_reason: 8 independent traditions agree · Cochrane reviewed but no drug approval · condition affects hundreds of millions

plants.modern_botanical: Bursera copallifera / B. bipinnata / B. simaruba (multiple species)

maya.source_text: Ritual of the Bacabs (Roys 1965); documented Maya ethnobotany
maya.original: Ix-pom — ku ts'o'okol u yik'nal nabal naab. Ku yilaj ti' máak ku k'iinikil.
maya.translation: Ix-pom (copal) — it extinguishes the burning of the skin [infected wound]. It is used to treat sick persons. Burned as incense in healing ceremonies and applied directly as resin to infected wounds and sores. The Maya specifically distinguished between copal burned as incense (ritual/respiratory use) and copal applied as resin (wound use) — recognising different preparations for different applications.
maya.preparation: Resin applied directly to wounds; burned as incense for respiratory and ritual use
maya.condition: Wound infection, respiratory conditions, ritual healing

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Book VI, cap. CVI — translated in this archive
cobo.original: De las demás especies de copal — eight species of copal-producing trees documented
cobo.translation: Eight distinct copal-producing tree species documented by Cobo across New Spain, each with antimicrobial and medicinal resin. Multiple species independently noted for treating dysentery when dissolved in water and cacao.
cobo.preparation: Resin dissolved in water and cacao (internal); burned as incense (inhalation)

modern_research.key_compound: Boswellic acids (Boswellia — related Burseraceae), alpha-pinene, limonene, incensole acetate
modern_research.summary: Burseraceae resins broadly show antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory activity. Incensole acetate (from frankincense/Boswellia) shows neuroprotective and anxiolytic effects in animal models — may explain ritual calming effects. Boswellic acids (related species) have clinical evidence for osteoarthritis and inflammatory bowel disease.
modern_research.significance: MAXIMUM MESOAMERICAN CONVERGENCE: Maya, Aztec (Badianus + Florentine), and Andean (Cobo) traditions all independently documented Burseraceae resins for wound infection and respiratory use. This represents the strongest within-Americas convergence signal in the archive. The connection to Greek/Roman frankincense (Boswellia, same family) extends the signal globally.

plants.evidence_authority: Boswellic acids — systematic reviews; Boswellia approved in EU as orphan drug for brain tumors
plants.priority_reason: 9 independent civilisations independently documented this · condition burden is critical · compound identified but no clinical trials

celtic_norse.source_text: Bald's Leechbook I (Cockayne vol.1, c.900 CE); Lacnunga
celtic_norse.original: Wið untrumnesse, genim þeos wyrt ðe man betonicam nemneþ, gebræd on buteran, smire mid.
celtic_norse.translation: For sickness, take this plant called betonica, cooked in butter, smear with it. For monstrous nocturnal visitors and frightful sights and dreams — betonica applied. For sore of eyes, for sore of ears, for dimness of eyes, for strong blood-running from the nose, for toothache, for sore of side, for sore of belly — betonica used throughout. The Leechbook devotes its entire first chapter to betonica, listing 29 specific medical indications — the most uses attributed to any single plant in the Anglo-Saxon medical tradition.
celtic_norse.preparation: Leaves cooked in butter and applied topically; decocted in ale or water for internal use; dried and powdered
celtic_norse.condition: Head pain, digestive disorders, respiratory, anxiety/nightmares, wound healing (29 documented indications)
celtic_norse.notes: Betony's 29 documented indications in Bald's Leechbook make it the most multi-purpose plant in Anglo-Saxon medicine. The inclusion of anxiety and nightmares among its indications ('monstrous nocturnal visitors') is noteworthy — betony has mild sedative activity that may account for its traditional use for sleep disturbance and anxiety.

greek_roman.source_text: Pliny, Naturalis Historia XXVII; Dioscorides De Materia Medica IV
greek_roman.original: Betonica — herba salutaris, omnibus morbis medetur.
greek_roman.translation: Betonica — a health-bringing herb, it cures all diseases. Roman soldiers used it as a general tonic and head medicine. Applied for headache, for bites of venomous animals, for fever.
greek_roman.preparation: Dried and powdered in wine; applied as poultice
greek_roman.condition: Headache, fever, snakebite, general tonic

modern_research.summary: Limited modern research. Rosmarinic acid (anti-inflammatory, antioxidant) is shared with rosemary, sage, and other Lamiaceae — likely responsible for some traditional effects. Stachydrine shows cardioprotective activity in animal models. Traditional use for anxiety and sleep disturbance not well studied despite historical prominence.
modern_research.significance: The convergence between Anglo-Saxon and Roman documentation of betony as a multi-purpose medicine — independently identified by two traditions for overlapping conditions — warrants more systematic investigation than it has received. The gap between its historical prominence and modern research attention makes it a priority research candidate.

plants.evidence_authority: Stachydrine — research stage; limited clinical data
plants.priority_reason: 6 independent civilisations independently documented this · condition burden is critical · compound identified but no clinical trials

tradition_names.north_american_indigenous: White man's footprint (multiple nations — arrived with Europeans)

celtic_norse.source_text: Bald's Leechbook (Cockayne vol.1, c.900 CE); Nine Herbs Charm (Lacnunga)
celtic_norse.original: Ond þu, wegbrade, wyrta modor, eastan openige, innan mihtigu; ofer ðe crætu curran, ofer ðe cwene reodan.
celtic_norse.translation: And you, waybread (plantain), mother of plants, open from the east, mighty within; over you carts have rolled, over you queens have ridden. [Nine Herbs Charm, Lacnunga]. Wound applications: leaves applied directly to wounds to stop bleeding and prevent infection. Leaf poultice for insect stings, burns. Leaf decoction drunk for cough and diarrhea. The Nine Herbs Charm positions waybread as one of the nine most important medicinal plants — alongside mugwort, stinging nettle, and cress.
celtic_norse.preparation: Fresh leaf applied directly to wounds; decocted for cough; chewed for mouth sores
celtic_norse.condition: Wound infection and bleeding, cough, diarrhea, insect stings

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II
greek_roman.translation: Plantain — cleanses and closes wounds. Leaves applied to old ulcers and wounds hasten healing. Juice drunk for bloody flux and for kidney disease.
greek_roman.preparation: Fresh leaves applied topically; juice extracted
greek_roman.condition: Wound healing, dysentery, kidney conditions

modern_research.summary: Aucubin shows anti-inflammatory activity. Allantoin promotes wound healing (also found in comfrey). Mucilage soothes mucous membranes — validates respiratory use. Multiple studies showing plantain leaf extract reduces wound healing time. Antimicrobial activity against common pathogens.
modern_research.significance: GLOBAL CONVERGENCE: Plantago major spread worldwide with European colonization — Native American peoples called it 'white man's footprint' because it appeared wherever Europeans settled, and then adopted it into their own medicine for identical uses (wound healing, cough). This makes Plantago a unique case: cross-tradition convergence happened in real time and was historically documented, creating a natural experiment confirming the wound-healing effect across independent observers.

plants.evidence_authority: Aucubin/Allantoin — Cochrane on Plantago for respiratory; wound healing RCTs
plants.priority_reason: 9 independent traditions agree · Cochrane reviewed but no drug approval · condition affects hundreds of millions

plants.modern_botanical: Ephedra species (most likely E. intermedia or E. sinica)

persian_zoroastrian.source_text: Avesta, Yasna hymns 9-11 (Hom Yasht); Vendidad; Bundahishn
persian_zoroastrian.original: Haoma — yō zaothrābyo paiti.vīsaiti | yō ashāum haomō fravaōiti. (Yasna 9.1) Haoma gave strength, Haoma gave victory, Haoma gave children.
persian_zoroastrian.translation: Haoma — you who come to the libations, you who are sacred haoma. Haoma, pressed by the mortar, mixed with milk and water, drunk in the sacred ritual. Haoma drives away death, gives strength to warriors, gives children to women, gives wisdom to the priest. The Hom Yasht (Yasna 9-11) dedicates three full hymns to haoma — more ritual attention than any other plant in the Avestan corpus. Bundahishn natural history: haoma grows on the mountain Alburz, the cosmic mountain. Its pressing and preparation require specific ritual conditions. Effects documented: increased mental clarity, reduced fatigue, heightened spiritual awareness, physical endurance.
persian_zoroastrian.preparation: Stems pressed by mortar, juice extracted, mixed with milk and water, consumed ritually
persian_zoroastrian.condition: Fatigue, weakness, spiritual purification, fertility, battle preparation
persian_zoroastrian.notes: The identification of haoma as Ephedra is not universal — alternative candidates include Peganum harmala (Syrian rue, containing harmaline — an MAO inhibitor), Sarcostemma species, or Cannabis. The debate mirrors the Vedic Soma controversy (same plant, different linguistic tradition). If haoma = Ephedra, the active compound is ephedrine — a stimulant consistent with the documented effects (energy, endurance, mental clarity). If haoma = Peganum harmala, the MAO-inhibiting beta-carbolines would explain the visionary/spiritual effects. The most pharmacologically satisfying interpretation may be a mixture: Ephedra providing stimulant effects, with MAO-inhibiting additions for the visionary component — a sophisticated empirical discovery paralleling ayahuasca's MAOi combination.

modern_research.key_compound: Ephedrine, pseudoephedrine (if Ephedra); harmaline, harmine (if Peganum harmala)
modern_research.summary: If Ephedra: ephedrine is pharmacologically confirmed as a CNS stimulant and bronchodilator — consistent with documented haoma effects of endurance and clarity. If Peganum harmala: MAO inhibition confirmed, with documented psychoactive effects. Both interpretations are pharmacologically consistent with ancient texts. Modern Ephedra still used in Kampo (maōu) and Chinese medicine.
modern_research.significance: Haoma represents the oldest documented ritual pharmacological plant in the world — the Avestan hymns date to at least 1200 BCE, potentially much older. The pharmacological sophistication implied by haoma's documented effects (regardless of exact species identification) represents knowledge accumulated over millennia of empirical observation at the dawn of recorded civilization.

plants.evidence_authority: Ephedrine — FDA approved (same compound as MMA-022)

philippine.source_text: Quisumbing, Medicinal Plants of the Philippines (1951); Philippine DOH registration
philippine.original: Ampalaya — iniluluto ang dahon at bunga para sa diabetes. Ginagamit din para sa mga bulate at lagnat.
philippine.translation: Ampalaya — leaves and fruit cooked and eaten for diabetes ('sugar in the urine'). Also used for intestinal worms and fever. The Philippine tradition distinguishes between the leaves (stronger bitterness, used medicinally as tea) and the fruit (eaten as vegetable, milder medicinal effect). DOH-registered for Type 2 diabetes adjunct treatment.
philippine.preparation: Leaves decocted as tea; fruit eaten raw or cooked; standardised extract tablets

chinese.original: 苦瓜 — 清熱解毒、明目、益気。治煩渇。
chinese.translation: Kugua — clears heat and toxins, brightens the eyes, benefits qi. Treats thirst and irritability. Chinese traditional medicine uses for the same blood-sugar modulating indication predate Philippine documentation.
chinese.preparation: Fruit eaten or decocted; seeds also used
chinese.condition: Heat conditions, thirst, blood sugar dysregulation

modern_research.summary: Multiple clinical trials including Philippine PIMS trial showing HbA1c reduction. Polypeptide-p has structural similarity to insulin. Charantin shown to stimulate insulin secretion. WHO traditional medicine guidelines cite ampalaya as having promising anti-diabetic evidence. Multiple meta-analyses: consistent blood glucose lowering effect, though large RCTs needed.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Philippine, Chinese, and Ayurvedic traditions independently documented bitter melon for blood sugar regulation — a triple convergence supporting a genuine pharmacological effect. The Philippine DOH formal registration for Type 2 diabetes represents one of the world's first government-backed traditional medicine approvals for a metabolic disease.

plants.evidence_authority: Charantin/Polypeptide-p — multiple RCTs; systematic review on bitter melon for T2DM
plants.priority_reason: 9 independent traditions agree · Cochrane reviewed but no drug approval · condition affects hundreds of millions

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book IV; Pliny NH Book XXV
greek_roman.original: μανδραγόρας — οἶνος ἐκ τῆς ῥίζης ποιούμενος κυάθου δοθεὶς πρὸ τομῆς ἢ καύσεως ἀναισθησίαν ποιεῖ
greek_roman.translation: Mandrake — wine made from the root, a cyathus given before cutting or cauterizing, produces insensibility. Used for those bitten by serpents and for those who cannot sleep. The bark taken in wine produces visions. Too large a dose is lethal — pine resin in wine is the antidote.
greek_roman.preparation: Root in wine — small dose for insensibility; moderate dose for vision; large dose lethal
greek_roman.condition: Surgical anesthesia, pain, insomnia, snakebite
greek_roman.notes: Earliest documented surgical anesthesia in European medical literature — 1,800 years before ether.

modern_research.summary: Same alkaloids as Datura (toloache) — WHO Essential Medicines. The ancient surgical anesthetic application is pharmacologically valid: scopolamine causes amnesia and sedation; hyoscyamine causes analgesia.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Greek/Roman, Islamic, and Anglo-Saxon traditions all document mandrake for surgical anesthesia and pain — same plant, same alkaloids as the Aztec toloache documented in the Badianus Manuscript. Maximum cross-tradition convergence for the surgical anesthesia indication.

plants.evidence_authority: Scopolamine — FDA approved (same as MMA-009); atropine WHO Essential Medicine

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book III
greek_roman.original: ἀνθεμίς — ἕψεται δὲ ἡ ῥίζα αὐτῆς εἰς λοετρόν, ὃ ἁρμόζει στρόφοις κοιλίας
greek_roman.translation: Chamomile — the root boiled and the decoction taken as a bath suits gripping pains of the belly. Taken in drink it helps those with kidney stones. The flowers in oil poured into the ears ease ear pain. Applied as poultice it ripens and disperses hard swellings.
greek_roman.preparation: Flowers decocted as bath and drink; flowers in oil for ears; poultice
greek_roman.condition: Digestive pain, kidney stones, ear pain, swellings, headache

modern_research.summary: Multiple RCTs for anxiety (apigenin — GABA-A receptor modulation), infant colic, and inflammatory conditions. Alpha-bisabolol: wound healing and anti-inflammatory. Chamomile tea: most widely consumed herbal tea globally.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Greek/Roman, Egyptian, Islamic, and Anglo-Saxon traditions all document chamomile for digestive pain and inflammation — one of the most globally convergent plants for these indications after willow bark and honey.

plants.evidence_authority: Apigenin — Cochrane on chamomile for anxiety, colic, GI; multiple RCTs
plants.priority_reason: 9 independent traditions agree · Cochrane reviewed but no drug approval · condition affects hundreds of millions

egyptian.source_text: Ebers Papyrus (c.1550 BCE), Papyrus Berlin, Bryan translation
egyptian.original: [Hieratic] kiki — remedy for driving away any disease of the bowels: the seeds of kiki, crush in water, drink for four days. The bowels will be completely cleared.
egyptian.translation: Kiki (castor) — remedy for driving away disease of the bowels: crush the seeds in water, drink for four days — the bowels will be completely cleared. Also: castor oil applied to wounds to prevent corruption; rubbed on the head to promote hair growth; applied to skin lesions. Among the most frequently mentioned plant medicines in the Ebers Papyrus — appearing in over 50 prescriptions.
egyptian.preparation: Seeds crushed in water for internal use; oil extracted for topical application
egyptian.condition: Constipation, wound care, skin disease, hair loss
egyptian.notes: Among the most-used plants in the Ebers Papyrus, appearing in over 50 different prescriptions — and still in modern pharmacopeias as a laxative. 3,500 years of continuous documented use.

modern_research.key_compound: Ricinoleic acid (in oil), ricin (in seed cake — toxic, never consumed)
modern_research.summary: Castor oil is still in modern pharmacopeias as an official laxative. Ricinoleic acid: prostaglandin-like activity stimulating intestinal motility. Topical use validated for wound healing and skin conditions. Ricin (separate from the oil) — highly toxic, subject of bioterrorism concerns; never present in properly extracted oil.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Egyptian, Greek/Roman, and Ayurvedic traditions all independently document castor for constipation. 3,500 years of documented laxative use — one of the longest unbroken therapeutic traditions in the archive.

plants.evidence_authority: Ricinoleic acid / Castor oil — FDA approved OTC laxative; in USP since 1820

west_african.source_text: Dalziel, Useful Plants of West Tropical Africa (1937); modern Nigerian ethnobotany
west_african.original: [Yoruba oral tradition] Orogbo — a di oogun fun ẹdọ, fun ẹdọforo, fun agbara ọkùnrin. Ki a jẹ fun iwosan.
west_african.translation: Orogbo (Bitter Kola) — medicine for the liver, for the lungs, for male strength. It is eaten for healing. Seeds chewed for cough, chest complaints, liver disease, and male fertility. Used across Yoruba, Igbo, and other West African traditions with consistent indications.
west_african.preparation: Seeds chewed raw; seed extract in water; bark decoction
west_african.condition: Respiratory conditions, liver disease, male fertility, general tonic

modern_research.summary: Kolaviron: documented hepatoprotective activity; anti-inflammatory; antioxidant. Clinical study (Annals of Thoracic Medicine, 2009): Garcinia kola extract improved lung function in respiratory patients. HIV: components studied for anti-HIV activity in vitro. One of the most pharmacologically promising underdeveloped West African plants.
modern_research.significance: Traditional use for respiratory and liver conditions validated by documented mechanisms. The hepatoprotective and bronchodilator activities offer pathways to formal clinical development for conditions with significant global disease burden.

plants.evidence_authority: Kolaviron — Phase 1/2 research; not yet approved
plants.priority_reason: 3 traditions agree · moderate disease burden · active compounds identified but uninvestigated clinically

north_american_indigenous.source_text: Moerman's Native American Ethnobotany Database — Cherokee, Iroquois, Ojibwe, Meskwaki
north_american_indigenous.original: [Cherokee and Iroquois oral tradition — documented by Moerman]
north_american_indigenous.translation: Cherokee: root used as tonic, aphrodisiac, and for headache. Iroquois: root tea for fever, digestive complaints, and female disorders. Ojibwe: root as tonic for the weak and elderly. Meskwaki: root for menstrual cramps. Documented by multiple independent nations across Eastern Woodlands.
north_american_indigenous.preparation: Root decocted as tea; root chewed
north_american_indigenous.condition: General tonic, fatigue, fever, digestive complaints, menstrual disorders

modern_research.key_compound: Ginsenosides Rb1, Rg1, Re (different profile from Asian ginseng)
modern_research.summary: Multiple trials for blood sugar regulation in Type 2 diabetes — positive results. Cognitive function in healthy adults — modest positive results. Immune modulation documented. American ginseng higher in Rb1 (sedating, anti-stress) vs Asian ginseng higher in Rg1 (stimulating).
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Panax ginseng documented in Chinese and Kampo; Panax quinquefolius independently documented by multiple North American nations — same genus independently identified on opposite sides of the Pacific with similar medicinal properties. One of the clearest examples of convergent pharmacological discovery across civilisations.

plants.evidence_authority: Ginsenosides — Cochrane on ginseng for T2DM, cognition; multiple RCTs
plants.priority_reason: 8 independent traditions agree · Cochrane reviewed but no drug approval · condition affects hundreds of millions

tradition_names.amazonian: Epená / Yakee (multiple nations — Yanomami, Tukano, Bora)

amazonian.source_text: Schultes, Plants of the Gods (1979); Schultes field documentation 1941-1953
amazonian.original: [Yanomami and Tukano oral tradition, documented by Schultes]
amazonian.translation: Epená — the bark resin of the virola tree is dried and ground into a fine powder, blown into the nostril through a long tube by an assistant. The healer uses epená to enter the trance state required to diagnose and treat disease, to communicate with the forest spirits, and to combat sorcery. Used across 14 Amazonian nations documented by Schultes — a remarkable internal convergence suggesting independent discovery of the same plant's properties across geographically separated groups.
amazonian.preparation: Bark resin dried and powdered; administered as nasal insufflation
amazonian.condition: Healing trance, diagnosis of illness cause, pain ritual management, sorcery combat

modern_research.summary: 5-MeO-DMT is among the most potent known serotonin 5-HT2A agonists. Active clinical research for treatment-resistant depression and PTSD (related to psilocybin and ayahuasca trials). Mechanism: profound short-duration serotonin agonism. Unlike ayahuasca, active without MAO inhibitor via insufflation route.
modern_research.significance: The 14-nation independent documentation of Virola across geographically separated Amazonian peoples represents strong internal convergence. The same plant, same preparation method, same application (healing trance) — discovered independently across the Amazon basin. The 5-HT2A agonist activity now being investigated for psychiatric medicine follows the same pattern as psilocybin and ayahuasca.

plants.evidence_authority: 5-MeO-DMT — Phase 1 trials; not approved
plants.priority_reason: 4 traditions agree · moderate disease burden · active compounds identified but uninvestigated clinically

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book VIII — extended non-target corpus
cobo.original: La chonta es una palma muy común en los llanos del Perú y en toda la tierra caliente de las Indias. Su fruto es muy nutritivo y sustentador. Los indios lo comen cocido y asado, y también hacen chicha de él.
cobo.translation: The chonta palm is very common in the lowlands of Peru and throughout the hot lands of the Indies. Its fruit is highly nutritious and sustaining. The Indians eat it cooked and roasted, and also make chicha from it. The palm heart is eaten for weakness and convalescence. The thorn-wood is used to make weapons and surgical tools — the sharpest natural cutting implement available in the lowland jungle.
cobo.preparation: Fruit cooked and eaten; chicha fermented from fruit; palm heart eaten

modern_research.key_compound: Carotenoids (highest documented in any palm fruit), vitamin A precursors, protein
modern_research.summary: Pupunha/peach palm has the highest carotenoid content of any commercially significant fruit — documented higher than carrot. Complete amino acid profile. Documented nutritional intervention in vitamin A deficiency programs in Brazil and Colombia. Food-medicine continuum: the Andean and Amazonian classification of chonta as simultaneously a staple food and medicine for weakness reflects the nutritional reality of its exceptional macro and micronutrient profile.
modern_research.significance: Represents the food-medicine continuum documented across multiple traditions — the chonta's documented use for weakness and convalescence is nutritionally validated by its exceptional carotenoid and protein content.

plants.evidence_authority: Carotenoids — nutritional intervention evidence; no specific drug approval
plants.priority_reason: 5 traditions agree · moderate disease burden · active compounds identified but uninvestigated clinically

egyptian.original: [Hieratic] saam — remedy for driving away worms in the belly
egyptian.translation: Saam (wormwood) — taken in beer to drive away worms in the belly and to cool fever. One of the most frequently prescribed plants in the Ebers Papyrus, appearing in over 20 prescriptions for intestinal parasites, fever, and digestive complaints.
egyptian.preparation: Crushed in beer or water; drunk for internal parasites

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book III; Pliny NH Book XXVII
greek_roman.translation: Wormwood — destroys intestinal worms, benefits the stomach. Dioscorides: taken in wine it warms the stomach and drives out worms. Applied with oil it eases pain of the ears. Drunk in decoction it helps those with jaundice. Pliny documents its use by Roman soldiers against intestinal parasites in the field.
greek_roman.preparation: Decocted in wine; applied in oil topically
greek_roman.condition: Intestinal parasites, digestive complaints, jaundice, ear pain

celtic_norse.original: Wermod — wið innon fynd, genim þas wyrte
celtic_norse.translation: Wermod (wormwood) — for internal enemies (parasites), take this plant. Bald's Leechbook prescribes wormwood for intestinal worms, digestive pain, and as a component in the Nine Herbs Charm. Used throughout Anglo-Saxon medicine for the same applications as Greek/Roman tradition — independent documentation of identical indications.
celtic_norse.preparation: Boiled in ale or water; drunk for internal conditions; applied as poultice

modern_research.summary: Artemisia absinthium contains thujone — a GABA receptor antagonist responsible for the psychoactive and toxic effects of absinthe. Sesquiterpene lactones show documented antiparasitic activity against Ascaris and other intestinal parasites. Absinthin is intensely bitter — the most bitter substance known — explaining its traditional use as a digestive bitter. Related species: Artemisia annua (sweet wormwood) is the source of artemisinin — Nobel Prize 2015.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Egyptian (1550 BCE), Greek (77 CE), and Anglo-Saxon (900 CE) traditions independently documented wormwood for intestinal parasites across 2,500 years — the same indication. The Nobel Prize for artemisinin came from the same genus. Artemisia absinthium's own antiparasitic compounds have not received the same clinical attention despite this extraordinary convergence signal.

plants.evidence_authority: Thujone pharmacology studied; absinthin documented; antiparasitic activity in vitro confirmed. No clinical trials for antiparasitic indication.
plants.priority_reason: 3 independent traditions agree across 2,500 years for intestinal parasites · Same genus as artemisinin (Nobel Prize) · Active compounds identified · No clinical trials for this indication

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book III; Pliny NH Book XX
greek_roman.translation: Rue — moves the menstrual flux; antidote for the bites of venomous creatures. Dioscorides: taken with wine it warms the stomach, relieves flatulence, stops hiccups, clears the eyesight. The juice dropped into the ears relieves pain. Historically notable: rue was included in the legendary Antidotum Mithridaticum — the poison antidote of Mithridates VI. Used by Roman physicians more than almost any other plant.
greek_roman.preparation: Fresh plant in wine; juice applied topically
greek_roman.condition: Menstrual disorders, snakebite antidote, digestive complaints, eye conditions

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book VI
cobo.original: La ruda es planta muy conocida y de grande uso en la medicina española y criolla del Perú.
cobo.translation: Rue is a very well-known plant of great use in Spanish and Creole medicine in Peru. Cobo documents its use by both Spanish colonists and indigenous Peruvians — the plant was introduced from Europe but rapidly adopted. Used for menstrual disorders, to expel intestinal worms, and as a general medicinal herb. Cobo notes that Andean healers combined it with indigenous plants for female reproductive conditions.
cobo.preparation: Decocted in water or applied as poultice

celtic_norse.original: Rude — wið wifmenn seocnysse, genim rude
celtic_norse.translation: Rue — for women's sickness, take rue. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents rue for the same gynecological applications as Greek/Roman tradition, demonstrating transmission through European medical tradition. Also used for ear pain and digestive complaints.
celtic_norse.preparation: Boiled in ale; applied as poultice
celtic_norse.condition: Menstrual disorders, ear pain, digestive complaints

modern_research.summary: Rue contains rutin — a flavonoid with documented anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and capillary-strengthening activity. Rutamarin and chalepensin show uterotonic activity (stimulates uterine contractions) — pharmacological basis for traditional use as an emmenagogue. Furocoumarins cause photosensitivity. Rue was historically used as an abortifacient — this is pharmacologically consistent with its uterotonic compounds. CAUTION: toxic in large doses; documented as dangerous in pregnancy.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Greek/Roman, Andean, and Anglo-Saxon traditions all independently documented rue for menstrual regulation — a triple convergence across three continents.
