The uterotonic pharmacology validates this specific traditional application. Rue's convergence for gynecological conditions is among the strongest in the archive for this therapeutic area.

plants.evidence_authority: Rutin approved as dietary supplement; uterotonic activity pharmacologically confirmed. No approved drug from rue compounds.
plants.priority_reason: 3 traditions across 3 continents agree on gynecological use · Uterotonic mechanism pharmacologically confirmed · CAUTION: potentially dangerous in pregnancy

egyptian.source_text: Ebers Papyrus (c.1550 BCE); also documented in workers' rations at Giza pyramid construction
egyptian.original: [Hieratic] iw — given to workers for strength; remedy for heart disease and tumours of the belly
egyptian.translation: Garlic — given to workers for strength and endurance; used as remedy for heart conditions and tumours of the belly. The Ebers Papyrus contains 22 garlic prescriptions — one of the most frequently mentioned plants. Historical note: Herodotus records that garlic, onion, and radish were inscribed on the Great Pyramid as food given to the pyramid workers, suggesting institutionalised medicinal food use.
egyptian.preparation: Eaten raw; decocted in water; applied topically
egyptian.condition: Cardiovascular conditions, digestive tumours, intestinal parasites, general strength

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II; Pliny NH Book XIX
greek_roman.translation: Garlic — dispels gas, is diuretic, destroys intestinal worms. Dioscorides: eaten it provokes urine, clears the arteries, benefits the cold stomach, is given for coughs and asthma. Applied it draws out leeches. Kills lice and nits when applied externally. The wild kind is more effective than the cultivated.
greek_roman.preparation: Eaten raw or cooked; applied topically
greek_roman.condition: Respiratory conditions, intestinal parasites, digestive gas, lice, cardiovascular

celtic_norse.original: Garlec — wið oman, wið wundum. Nim garlec, gesupan on ealu.
celtic_norse.translation: Garlic — for erysipelas (skin infection), for wounds. Take garlic, steeped in ale. Bald's Leechbook contains the famous garlic-based eye infection remedy validated by UCL researchers in 2015 — combining garlic, onion/leek, wine, and ox bile in a brass vessel — which showed activity against MRSA in modern laboratory testing.
celtic_norse.preparation: Steeped in ale; combined with onion, wine, and ox bile for eye infections
celtic_norse.condition: Skin infections, wound infection, eye infections (notably MRSA-active preparation)

modern_research.summary: Allicin — the primary active compound — is a potent broad-spectrum antimicrobial with documented activity against gram-positive bacteria including MRSA, gram-negative bacteria, fungi, and viruses. Cardiovascular: multiple Cochrane reviews confirm modest but real reduction in blood pressure and LDL cholesterol. UCL 2015: the Bald's Leechbook garlic-onion-wine-bile preparation showed 90%+ kill rate against MRSA biofilms — more effective than modern antibiotics against the biofilm form. Antiparasitic: allicin active against Giardia and Cryptosporidium.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Egyptian (1550 BCE), Greek (77 CE), and Anglo-Saxon (900 CE) traditions all independently documented garlic for infection and cardiovascular conditions across 2,500 years. The UCL validation of the Anglo-Saxon MRSA preparation is one of the most dramatic confirmations of traditional medicine's pharmacological accuracy in this archive — a 1,000-year-old recipe outperforming modern antibiotics against drug-resistant bacteria.

plants.evidence_authority: Cochrane review: garlic for hypertension and cardiovascular risk — modest but real effect confirmed. UCL 2015: Bald's Leechbook MRSA study published in Scientific Reports.
plants.priority_reason: 3 traditions across 2,500 years · UCL validated MRSA activity of 1,000-year-old preparation · Cochrane evidence for cardiovascular · Antibiotic resistance crisis makes allicin derivatives urgent research priority

egyptian.translation: Fennel seed prescribed in the Ebers Papyrus for digestive complaints and to relieve flatulence and abdominal cramping. Appears in compound prescriptions alongside coriander and cumin.
egyptian.preparation: Seeds decocted in water or beer

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book III; Pliny NH Book XX
greek_roman.translation: Fennel — promotes milk production, is diuretic, benefits the stomach. The seed taken in drink moves the urine and the menstrual flux. Applied with honey it sharpens the eyesight. The root decocted in wine and drunk helps those with pain of the kidneys and bladder.
greek_roman.preparation: Seed in drink; root decocted in wine; applied with honey
greek_roman.condition: Lactation, menstrual disorders, kidney complaints, eye conditions, digestive

celtic_norse.source_text: Bald's Leechbook (c.900 CE); Anglo-Saxon Nine Herbs Charm (Lacnunga)
celtic_norse.original: Finule — an ðæra nigon wyrta. Wiþ fefor, wiþ feondes costnunge.
celtic_norse.translation: Fennel — one of the Nine Herbs. Against fever, against the devil's temptation. One of the nine most important plants in Anglo-Saxon medicine, used for fever and digestive complaints. The inclusion in the Nine Herbs Charm alongside plantain and wormwood confirms its central status.
celtic_norse.preparation: Boiled in water or ale; applied topically

modern_research.key_compound: Trans-anethole (80-90% of essential oil), fenchone, estragole
modern_research.summary: Trans-anethole: antispasmodic activity on smooth muscle — pharmacological basis for traditional digestive use. Oestrogenic activity (structurally similar to oestrogen) — pharmacological basis for lactation-promoting and menstrual-regulating traditional use. Multiple studies confirm efficacy for infant colic. Cochrane: insufficient evidence for formal recommendation but consistent positive signals.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Three independent traditions (Egyptian, Greek, Anglo-Saxon) documented fennel for digestive complaints and menstrual disorders. The oestrogenic and antispasmodic mechanisms pharmacologically validate both applications. Fennel is now one of the most widely used herbal medicines globally — estimated 1 billion people use it — making it one of the most successful traditional medicine plants in current practice.

plants.evidence_authority: GRAS status (FDA); widely used as herbal medicine; clinical evidence for infant colic. No approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: 3 traditions agree · Oestrogenic mechanism validated · Widely used globally · Formal clinical development not pursued

egyptian.original: [Hieratic] mehyt — used in poultices and digestive prescriptions
egyptian.translation: Linseed prescribed in the Ebers Papyrus for wound poultices, digestive complaints, and to soothe inflamed conditions. Appears in multiple compound prescriptions alongside honey and other plant medicines.
egyptian.preparation: Ground seeds applied as poultice; decocted in water for internal use
egyptian.condition: Wound inflammation, digestive complaints, respiratory conditions

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II; Pliny NH Book XIX
greek_roman.translation: Linseed — softening, digestive, ripens abscesses. Dioscorides: boiled in water with honey and pepper it helps those with coughs. Applied as a poultice it ripens and softens hard swellings. The oil taken in drink loosens the belly. Applied with natron it removes skin blemishes.
greek_roman.preparation: Boiled with honey; applied as poultice; oil taken internally
greek_roman.condition: Respiratory conditions, swellings, constipation, skin conditions

celtic_norse.original: Lin — wið oman, wið wunde forswollenre
celtic_norse.translation: Flax — for erysipelas (skin infection), for swollen wounds. Anglo-Saxon medicine uses linseed poultices for infected and inflamed wounds, consistent with Greek/Roman documentation.
celtic_norse.preparation: Ground seeds applied as poultice

modern_research.key_compound: Alpha-linolenic acid (ALA, omega-3), lignans (secoisolariciresinol diglucoside), mucilage
modern_research.summary: Linseed is one of the richest plant sources of alpha-linolenic acid (omega-3 fatty acid) and plant lignans. Multiple RCTs for cardiovascular risk reduction — significant reduction in LDL cholesterol. Lignans: phytooestrogenic activity; epidemiological evidence for reduced breast cancer risk. Mucilage: soothing effect on inflamed mucous membranes validates respiratory and digestive traditional use. FDA allows qualified health claim for flaxseed and cardiovascular disease.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Egyptian, Greek/Roman, and Anglo-Saxon traditions all documented linseed for inflammation and wound healing. The mucilage mechanism directly validates the poultice application. Omega-3 content validates anti-inflammatory traditional use. Linseed is now one of the most commercially successful traditional medicine plants globally.

plants.evidence_authority: Cochrane review on omega-3 for cardiovascular risk; FDA qualified health claim for flaxseed.

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II; Pliny NH
greek_roman.translation: Leek — good for coughs; astringent. Dioscorides: the juice mixed with honey applied to the nostrils stops nosebleeds. Eaten it benefits those with coughs. Applied with honey and natron it stops the flow of menstrual blood when excessive. The seed taken in drink moves urine.
greek_roman.preparation: Juice mixed with honey; eaten; seed in drink
greek_roman.condition: Respiratory conditions, nosebleed, menstrual disorders, urinary

celtic_norse.source_text: Bald's Leechbook (c.900 CE) — famously part of the MRSA recipe
celtic_norse.original: Leac — nim leac and garleac, begean emela dæl
celtic_norse.translation: Leek — take leek and garlic, equal parts of both. The famous Bald's Leechbook eye infection recipe (validated by UCL 2015 against MRSA) calls for leek/onion alongside garlic. The leek component contributes additional allicin compounds to the antimicrobial preparation.
celtic_norse.preparation: Combined with garlic, wine, and ox bile

persian_zoroastrian.source_text: Vendidad (Zoroastrian ritual text) — plant medicine sections
persian_zoroastrian.original: [Avestan] — leek documented among the most important medicinal plants of the Persian world
persian_zoroastrian.translation: The leek is documented in the Zoroastrian tradition as one of the first plants created by Ahura Mazda for the healing of humanity. Used for respiratory conditions and as a general tonic — consistent with Greek/Roman documentation.

modern_research.key_compound: Allicin (lower concentration than garlic), kaempferol, quercetin, folate
modern_research.summary: Leek contains similar but lower concentrations of allicin compounds than garlic. Kaempferol: documented anti-inflammatory and antiproliferative activity in multiple cell studies. Folate: critical for DNA synthesis and neural tube development. As part of the UCL Bald's Leechbook MRSA preparation, leek contributes allicin compounds to the antimicrobial synergy. Limited independent clinical research on leek specifically.
modern_research.significance: Leek's primary modern interest is as a component of the UCL-validated Bald's Leechbook MRSA preparation. The three-tradition convergence for respiratory use is consistent with the allicin mechanism documented for garlic.

plants.evidence_authority: Component of UCL MRSA-active preparation; allicin pharmacology documented; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: 3 traditions agree · Component of UCL-validated MRSA preparation · Kaempferol antiproliferative research ongoing

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book VI
cobo.original: El lentisco es árbol muy conocido en la medicina española. Su resina tiene muchas virtudes para el estómago y los dientes.
cobo.translation: The mastic tree is a very well-known tree in Spanish medicine. Its resin has many virtues for the stomach and the teeth. Cobo documents that mastic resin was brought to the Americas by Spanish colonists who relied on it heavily — both for digestive complaints common in the colonial diet and for dental care. Indigenous healers adopted it and combined it with local Andean plants for digestive preparations.
cobo.preparation: Resin chewed directly; dissolved in wine for internal use
cobo.condition: Stomach complaints, dental disease, respiratory infections

celtic_norse.source_text: Anglo-Saxon medical texts and later medieval herbals
celtic_norse.original: Lentiscus — wið toðece, wið wambe sare
celtic_norse.translation: Mastic — for toothache, for stomach pain. Anglo-Saxon and medieval European medicine documents mastic resin as one of the primary treatments for dental pain and digestive complaints. The resin was traded across the Mediterranean and into Northern Europe, representing one of the earliest documented pharmaceutical trade routes.
celtic_norse.preparation: Chewed directly; applied to teeth

modern_research.key_compound: Alpha-pinene, beta-myrcene, mastic acid, tirucallol, oleanolic acid
modern_research.summary: Chios mastic (from Pistacia lentiscus var. chia) has been extensively studied. Multiple RCTs for Helicobacter pylori eradication — significant antibacterial activity against H. pylori, the primary cause of peptic ulcers. This directly validates the ancient digestive use. Oral health: randomised trial showed mastic chewing gum reduces oral bacteria and plaque significantly. Anti-inflammatory: inhibits IL-1β and TNF-α.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Colonial Andean and Anglo-Saxon traditions both document mastic for the same indications — digestive and dental. The H. pylori eradication data is particularly compelling: traditional use for stomach complaints is now understood to be treating bacterial gastritis. Mastic is one of the most rigorously studied traditional gum resins and shows a clear pathway from traditional use to therapeutic application.

plants.evidence_authority: Systematic review on mastic for H. pylori and peptic ulcer — positive results. Multiple RCTs for oral health.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions document identical uses · H. pylori RCT evidence strong · Dental application validated · Underutilised given strength of evidence

tradition_names.celtic_norse: Pipor (Old English — traded into Northern Europe)

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II; Pliny NH Book XII
greek_roman.translation: Pepper — warming, diuretic, digestive. Dioscorides: both kinds of pepper are warming, diuretic, and digestive. They break up intestinal gas, are beneficial for those chilled and shivering, clear the eyesight when rubbed on eyelids. Given for tetanus and epilepsy in wine. Applied with honey they clear corneal film. Pliny: pepper was among the most valuable trade commodities in Rome — pepper was among the ransom demanded by Alaric when he sacked Rome in 410 CE.
greek_roman.preparation: Taken in wine; applied with honey to eyes; taken with water for digestive complaints
greek_roman.condition: Digestive complaints, chills, eye conditions, tetanus

celtic_norse.original: Pipor — wið cyle, wið innonfærld, nim pipor on ealaþ
celtic_norse.translation: Pepper — for chills, for internal ailment, take pepper in ale. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents pepper for identical warming and digestive applications as Greek/Roman tradition. Pepper was highly valued in medieval Europe — archaeological finds at Anglo-Saxon sites confirm it was traded north from the Mediterranean.
celtic_norse.preparation: Mixed in ale or wine

modern_research.key_compound: Piperine (5-9% by weight), piperic acid, essential oils
modern_research.summary: Piperine: primary bioactive compound. Documented mechanisms: (1) thermogenic — activates TRPV1 (heat receptor), explaining traditional 'warming' use; (2) bioavailability enhancer — piperine inhibits drug-metabolising enzymes, dramatically increasing absorption of other compounds (notably curcumin — 2000% increase). Anti-inflammatory: inhibits NF-κB. Antimicrobial: moderate activity against gram-positive bacteria. Piperine is now widely used in supplement formulations specifically to increase bioavailability of other compounds.
modern_research.significance: Black pepper's most important modern pharmacological finding — that piperine dramatically increases the bioavailability of other compounds — was not documented in traditional medicine but may explain why pepper appears as an additive in so many traditional compound preparations. The ancient formulators may have empirically discovered that pepper made other medicines 'work better' without knowing why.

plants.evidence_authority: Piperine bioavailability enhancement well-documented; GRAS status; no approved drug for therapeutic indication.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · Piperine bioavailability mechanism suggests value in combination formulations · Underinvestigated as therapeutic compound independent of its role as bioavailability enhancer

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II; Pliny NH
greek_roman.translation: Radish — crushes and expels kidney stones. Dioscorides: eaten before other food it helps the stomach to digest and prevents food from lying heavy. Taken in vinegar it removes dark spots on the face. Applied with honey and rue it is good for those who cough. The juice dropped into the ears stops pain. Pliny devotes an unusual amount of attention to radish — 43 medical uses, more than almost any other plant.
greek_roman.preparation: Eaten before meals; juice applied topically; taken in vinegar
greek_roman.condition: Digestive complaints, kidney stones, respiratory, ear pain, skin conditions

celtic_norse.original: Raedic — wið stanas on blæddran, wið magan seocnysse
celtic_norse.translation: Radish — for stones in the bladder, for sickness of the stomach. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents radish for urinary/kidney stone complaints and digestive conditions, consistent with Greek/Roman documentation.
celtic_norse.preparation: Eaten; decocted in water

modern_research.key_compound: Sulforaphane, glucosinolates (glucoraphasatin), isothiocyanates, anthocyanins (in red varieties)
modern_research.summary: Glucosinolates: the same class of compounds responsible for broccoli's cancer-preventive properties — produced in radish in significant quantities. Sulforaphane induces phase 2 detoxification enzymes and has documented antiproliferative activity. Traditional use for kidney stones has some pharmacological plausibility via increased urine flow and possible calcium oxalate crystal inhibition. Limited clinical data on radish specifically.
modern_research.significance: Radish's 43 documented uses in Pliny — more than almost any other plant — combined with Anglo-Saxon documentation suggests it was among the most important European medicinal plants. The glucosinolate content connects it to the most active area of current brassica cancer research. Underinvestigated given the convergence and the documented compound potency.

plants.evidence_authority: Glucosinolate pharmacology documented; sulforaphane active research; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · Glucosinolates connect to active cancer research · 43 uses in Pliny suggests high traditional value · Formal clinical development not pursued

tradition_names.egyptian: Referenced in later Ptolemaic period texts

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book IV
cobo.original: El maíz es la planta más importante de las Indias. Los indios hacen de él chicha, que es su bebida principal y tiene virtudes medicinales.
cobo.translation: Maize is the most important plant of the Indies. The Indians make chicha from it, which is their principal drink and has medicinal virtues. Cobo documents maize in extraordinary detail — the silk (corn silk) was used for urinary complaints; fermented chicha was prescribed for digestive disorders; roasted corn applied as a poultice for skin conditions. The distinction between food and medicine is particularly blurred with maize in Andean tradition.
cobo.preparation: Corn silk decocted for urinary conditions; fermented as chicha; roasted for poultices
cobo.condition: Urinary complaints, digestive disorders, skin conditions

egyptian.source_text: Later Egyptian agricultural and medical texts
egyptian.original: [Ptolemaic period — maize not native to Egypt; documented after trans-Atlantic contact]
egyptian.translation: Note: Maize is a New World crop and was not available in Egypt until after 1492. Its appearance in Egyptian records represents post-Columbian adoption. This convergence is therefore transmission-based, not independent.
egyptian.preparation: Consumed as food; corn silk decocted

modern_research.key_compound: Corn silk (Zea mays stigma): maysin, stigmasterol, sitosterol; kernel: zeaxanthin, lutein, ferulic acid
modern_research.summary: Corn silk: multiple studies showing diuretic activity and positive effects on kidney stone prevention — directly validates Cobo's documentation of urinary use. Zeaxanthin and lutein: documented protective effects against macular degeneration. Ferulic acid: significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity. Corn silk is now sold as a herbal supplement for urinary health in many countries.
modern_research.significance: Maize corn silk as a urinary medicine is one of the clearest examples of traditional pharmacological knowledge surviving into modern herbal practice. Cobo's documentation is among the first written records of this application in the New World.

plants.evidence_authority: Corn silk diuretic activity documented; zeaxanthin/lutein in FDA-reviewed supplements; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: Corn silk urinary application validated · Zeaxanthin/lutein eye health evidence strong · Food-medicine continuum underinvestigated

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book III; Pliny NH
greek_roman.translation: Mint — benefits the stomach; good for the mouth. Dioscorides: dried mint mixed with vinegar stops hiccups. Drunk in water it stops vomiting. Applied it eases headache and stops bleeding from wounds. The smell alone cheers the mind and refreshes the senses.
greek_roman.preparation: Dried in vinegar; drunk in water; applied topically; inhaled as aroma
greek_roman.condition: Digestive complaints, headache, wound bleeding, nausea

celtic_norse.original: Minte — wið heafod sare, wið wambe sare
celtic_norse.translation: Mint — for headache, for stomach pain. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents mint for digestive and head complaints, consistent with Greek/Roman documentation. Used extensively throughout medieval European medicine.
celtic_norse.preparation: Boiled in water or ale; applied as compress

modern_research.key_compound: Menthol, menthone, menthyl acetate, rosmarinic acid
modern_research.summary: Menthol: calcium channel blocker producing cooling sensation and antispasmodic effect on smooth muscle. Peppermint oil capsules: multiple RCTs show significant reduction in IBS symptoms — one of the best-evidenced herbal treatments for any condition. Menthol inhaled: decongestant and cooling effect on airways documented. Topical: analgesic via TRPM8 receptor activation (cold receptor).
modern_research.significance: Mint is one of the most pharmacologically validated traditional plants — its primary compound menthol is in hundreds of approved pharmaceutical products (cough drops, topical analgesics, digestive aids). The traditional use for digestive complaints and headache is directly validated by menthol's documented mechanisms.

plants.evidence_authority: Peppermint oil: FDA approved as OTC digestive aid; menthol approved in multiple topical and inhalation products; in USP

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book III
greek_roman.translation: Dill — dispels gas, promotes milk production. The seed boiled and drunk stops hiccups. Applied as a fomentation it eases pain of the stomach. The oil of the seed is warming. Burned as incense it stops hiccups. Taken in large quantities it diminishes sight and dulls the senses.
greek_roman.preparation: Seed boiled; applied as fomentation; oil extracted
greek_roman.condition: Flatulence, lactation, stomach pain, hiccups

celtic_norse.original: Dile — wið wambe sare, wið ceacadle
celtic_norse.translation: Dill — for stomach pain, for hiccups. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents dill for identical applications as Greek/Roman — digestive complaints and hiccups. One of the more common plants in Anglo-Saxon medicinal use.
celtic_norse.preparation: Boiled in water or ale

modern_research.key_compound: Carvone (40-60% of essential oil), limonene, dill ether, flavonoids
modern_research.summary: Carvone: antispasmodic activity on smooth muscle — directly validates traditional use for digestive cramping and flatulence. Also shown to inhibit carcinogen-activating enzymes. Multiple studies on dill for infant colic and digestive complaints — positive results. Dill seed extract: preliminary evidence for blood glucose reduction.
modern_research.significance: Dill's convergence between Greek/Roman and Anglo-Saxon traditions for digestive use is consistent with the antispasmodic pharmacology of carvone. One of many Apiaceae family plants (alongside fennel, caraway, anise) where traditional digestive use is validated by carbone-family compounds.

plants.evidence_authority: GRAS status; antispasmodic activity documented; infant colic studies positive; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · Antispasmodic mechanism validated · Infant colic evidence promising · Formal development not pursued

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II; Pliny NH Book XVIII
greek_roman.translation: Wheat flour — softening, digestive. Applied in a poultice with boiled figs it mollifies and ripens swellings. Bread crumbs chewed and applied draw out splinters. The water in which wheat has been boiled is good for the throat when gargled. Old lard rendered with wheat flour applied warm relieves pain of the joints and helps those with gout and sciatica.
greek_roman.preparation: Flour as poultice; bread crumbs applied; wheat water gargled; flour with lard for joints
greek_roman.condition: Swellings, splinters, sore throat, joint pain, wound healing

persian_zoroastrian.source_text: Bundahishn (Zoroastrian creation text) — agricultural and medicinal plants
persian_zoroastrian.original: [Avestan/Middle Persian] gandom — from the original creation of Ahura Mazda for the sustenance and healing of humanity
persian_zoroastrian.translation: Wheat — created by Ahura Mazda as one of the primary plants for human sustenance and health. The Bundahishn places wheat among the most sacred of plants, created with both nutritional and medicinal properties. Used medicinally for digestive complaints and as wound poultice material.
persian_zoroastrian.preparation: Flour applied as poultice; consumed as bread
persian_zoroastrian.condition: General nutrition, wound healing, digestive complaints

modern_research.key_compound: Gluten (structural protein), arabinoxylan, ferulic acid, wheat germ oil (vitamin E)
modern_research.summary: Wheat bran: documented reduction in cardiovascular risk and Type 2 diabetes (dietary fibre effect). Wheat germ oil: high vitamin E content with antioxidant activity. The traditional poultice use of wheat flour has a plausible mechanism — the starch forms a demulcent barrier that maintains wound moisture and may provide mild antimicrobial activity via fermentation products. Note: coeliac disease affects ~1% of population — wheat is also the most important dietary allergen.
modern_research.significance: Wheat's medical use is complicated by the modern understanding that gluten causes serious disease in a significant population — a reminder that traditional medicine's 'foods are medicines' paradigm cuts both ways. The convergence between Greek/Roman and Persian traditions for wound poultice use is consistent with the demulcent mechanism.

plants.evidence_authority: Dietary fibre/cardiovascular FDA qualified health claim; wheat germ oil supplements established; gluten pathology well-documented.

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II
greek_roman.translation: Beet — laxative, promotes wound healing. The juice of the black beet taken as a drink is good for those bitten by serpents. Applied to the head it kills lice and nits. The leaves applied with honey are good for foul ulcers. The juice of the root dropped into the ear relieves ringing. Pliny notes that beet was prized above all other vegetables by the Pythagoreans for its medicinal properties.
greek_roman.preparation: Juice drunk; leaves applied as poultice; juice dropped into ears
greek_roman.condition: Constipation, snakebite, lice, wound healing, ear conditions

celtic_norse.original: Bete — wið innoðes yfelum, wið þa þe blod spiwað
celtic_norse.translation: Beet — for ailments of the bowels, for those who spit blood. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents beet for digestive and respiratory-related blood loss — consistent with modern understanding of beet's effect on blood and circulation.
celtic_norse.preparation: Eaten; decocted in water

modern_research.summary: Dietary nitrates in beetroot: converted to nitric oxide in the body, producing significant vasodilation. Multiple RCTs showing beetroot juice lowers blood pressure and improves athletic endurance. Betaine: reduces homocysteine levels (cardiovascular risk factor). Betalains: anti-inflammatory, antioxidant. The Anglo-Saxon observation that beet helps 'those who spit blood' (haemoptysis) may reflect the traditional use of beet to improve cardiovascular circulation.
modern_research.significance: Beet nitrate → nitric oxide → vasodilation is now one of the best-evidenced dietary interventions for blood pressure. The traditional use for blood-related conditions across both traditions is pharmacologically plausible. Beetroot juice is now widely used as a sports performance supplement based on clinical evidence — validating the traditional intuition about its effects on blood and circulation.

plants.evidence_authority: Systematic review on beetroot juice for hypertension and exercise performance — positive results. Multiple RCTs.

tradition_names.celtic_norse: Fenograecum (from Latin — used in Anglo-Saxon texts)

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II; Pliny NH
greek_roman.translation: Fenugreek — softening, digestive, promotes milk production. Dioscorides: the seed decocted with dates and raisins eases coughs. Applied as a fomentation it softens hard swellings. The seed taken in wine moves the menstrual flux. Used by Roman physicians for a remarkable range of conditions — Pliny documents its use for fever, digestive complaints, and female reproductive health.
greek_roman.preparation: Seed decocted; applied as fomentation; taken in wine
greek_roman.condition: Coughs, swellings, menstrual disorders, digestive complaints, fever

celtic_norse.source_text: Anglo-Saxon herbals referencing the Latin tradition
celtic_norse.original: Fenograecum — wið lifera sare, wið wambe sare
celtic_norse.translation: Fenugreek — for liver pain, for stomach pain. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents fenugreek for the same digestive and anti-inflammatory applications as the Greek/Roman tradition, transmitted through Latin medical texts.
celtic_norse.preparation: Decocted in water or ale
celtic_norse.condition: Liver conditions, stomach pain, digestive complaints

modern_research.summary: 4-hydroxyisoleucine: unique amino acid that stimulates insulin secretion — multiple clinical trials showing significant reduction in blood glucose in Type 2 diabetes. Trigonelline: precursor to nicotinic acid (vitamin B3); documented neuroprotective activity. Galactomannan: soluble fibre that slows glucose absorption. Saponins: oestrogenic activity, explains milk production and menstrual effects. Multiple RCTs for Type 2 diabetes — positive results.
modern_research.significance: Fenugreek is now one of the most clinically investigated traditional plants for Type 2 diabetes — a condition not documented in ancient medicine as such, but for which fenugreek was empirically identified as beneficial for related symptoms (excessive thirst, weakness). The insulin-stimulating mechanism of 4-hydroxyisoleucine is unique to fenugreek among known plants.

plants.evidence_authority: Systematic reviews on fenugreek for Type 2 diabetes — positive results. Multiple RCTs for blood glucose control.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · 4-hydroxyisoleucine insulin stimulation unique in plant kingdom · Type 2 diabetes RCT evidence · Underutilised given mechanism specificity

tradition_names.egyptian: Iw (documented in Egyptian agricultural medical texts)

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II; Pythagorean dietary texts
greek_roman.translation: Fava bean — astringent, produces gas. Applied as a poultice with vinegar it reduces swellings. The meal of beans mixed with vinegar and applied removes dark spots from the skin. Taken in drink it moves urine. Pythagoreans famously prohibited the eating of fava beans — possibly connected to the medical observation that fava beans cause haemolytic anaemia in certain individuals (now understood as G6PD deficiency).
greek_roman.preparation: Applied as poultice; meal mixed with vinegar
greek_roman.condition: Swellings, skin blemishes, urinary complaints

egyptian.source_text: Ebers Papyrus references; Egyptian agricultural and dietary texts
egyptian.original: [Hieratic] — beans in digestive and wound prescriptions
egyptian.translation: Fava beans documented in Egyptian medicine for digestive complaints and as poultice material. Among the oldest cultivated plants in the world — cultivated in Egypt since 6000 BCE.
egyptian.preparation: Cooked and eaten; applied as poultice

modern_research.key_compound: L-DOPA (levodopa — 0.5-2.5% of dry weight), vicine, convicine, quercetin
modern_research.summary: Fava beans contain the highest natural concentration of L-DOPA of any commonly eaten food. L-DOPA is the primary treatment for Parkinson's disease — the pharmaceutical form (as Levodopa) is an FDA-approved drug and WHO Essential Medicine. Multiple studies show fava bean consumption significantly raises plasma L-DOPA levels. CAUTION: vicine and convicine cause haemolytic anaemia in G6PD-deficient individuals (favism) — this risk was empirically observed by the Pythagoreans.
modern_research.significance: Fava beans represent a remarkable case where the most important pharmacological compound (L-DOPA for Parkinson's disease) was not the one traditional medicine was using it for — yet the plant was correctly identified as medicinally significant across multiple traditions. The Pythagorean prohibition may represent the oldest documented pharmacogenomic observation — recognising that this plant causes harm in specific individuals.

plants.evidence_authority: L-DOPA (Levodopa) — FDA approved for Parkinson's disease; WHO Essential Medicine. Fava beans as natural source of L-DOPA documented.

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book IV; Pliny NH
greek_roman.translation: Henbane — narcotic; for toothache. The seed drunk in wine makes men insane and similar to drunkards for a time. Applied to the forehead with oil of rose it causes sleep. Used as a fumigation it is good for pain of the teeth. Applied to swellings and inflammations it reduces them. But used too freely it brings madness and even death.
greek_roman.preparation: Seed in wine (narcotic); fumigation for dental pain; applied in oil of rose for sleep
greek_roman.condition: Surgical sedation, toothache, pain, insomnia, swellings

celtic_norse.original: Belene — wið toðwærce, wið sar. Drinc on ealaþ mid belene.
celtic_norse.translation: Henbane — for toothache, for pain. Drink in ale with henbane. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents henbane for the same narcotic and pain-relieving applications as Greek/Roman tradition. Used in 'dwale' — the Anglo-Saxon surgical anesthetic preparation — alongside hemlock, opium poppy, and mandrake. The consistent documentation across both traditions for the same applications demonstrates transmission through the European medical tradition.
celtic_norse.preparation: Seed in ale for pain; combined with other narcotics for surgical anesthesia

modern_research.summary: The same alkaloid family as mandrake (Mandragora) and Datura. Hyoscyamine: anticholinergic agent — FDA approved as antispasmodic (Levsin). Scopolamine: FDA approved for motion sickness and nausea (Transderm Scop patch) and for pre-operative sedation. Atropine: WHO Essential Medicine, used for cardiac emergencies, nerve agent antidotes, and ophthalmic dilation. All three alkaloids are pharmaceutically produced and widely used.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Greek/Roman and Anglo-Saxon traditions both document henbane for surgical anesthesia and pain — the same application. The Solanaceae family (henbane, mandrake, Datura, belladonna) produced some of the most important pharmaceutical compounds ever isolated — all from plants documented in this archive across multiple traditions.

plants.evidence_authority: Hyoscyamine (Levsin) FDA approved; Scopolamine (Transderm Scop) FDA approved; Atropine WHO Essential Medicine.

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II; Pliny NH
greek_roman.translation: Cress — warming, thinning, diuretic. Dioscorides: taken in drink with vinegar it helps those with enlarged spleen. Applied with honey it removes dark skin patches. Taken with vinegar it helps those with chronic cough. The seed taken in drink provokes urine. Pliny: cress was Hippocrates' remedy for clearing the respiratory passages — documented as being eaten with vinegar by legionaries.
greek_roman.preparation: Taken in drink with vinegar; applied with honey; seed in drink
greek_roman.condition: Spleen conditions, respiratory, skin patches, diuretic

celtic_norse.source_text: Bald's Leechbook and Lacnunga — cress as an important medicinal plant
celtic_norse.original: Cærse — wið þa adl þe men hataþ cancre, wið wamb sare
celtic_norse.translation: Cress — for the disease men call cancer (possibly any ulcerous condition), for belly pain. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents cress for ulcerating conditions and digestive complaints, consistent with the Greek/Roman tradition.
celtic_norse.preparation: Applied as poultice; boiled in water

modern_research.key_compound: Isothiocyanates (PEITC — phenethyl isothiocyanate), glucosinolates, beta-carotene, vitamin C
modern_research.summary: PEITC (phenethyl isothiocyanate): documented Phase 2 enzyme induction and antiproliferative activity against cancer cell lines — one of the most studied dietary isothiocyanates. Multiple epidemiological studies connecting watercress consumption to reduced cancer risk. Clinical trial (2011): watercress supplementation significantly reduced DNA damage in lymphocytes. Connects to the Brassica family's broader cancer-prevention evidence base (shared with broccoli, cabbage, radish).
modern_research.significance: Watercress's traditional documentation for 'the disease men call cancer' — while possibly referring to any ulcerating condition — is pharmacologically interesting given the strong modern cancer-preventive evidence for PEITC. Whether the traditional observation reflected genuine anti-tumour activity or merely the treatment of surface ulcers is uncertain, but the convergence between two traditions and the documented isothiocyanate activity makes this a notable candidate.

plants.evidence_authority: PEITC cancer prevention research ongoing; clinical trial evidence for DNA damage reduction; GRAS; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · PEITC cancer prevention mechanism documented · Clinical trial evidence for DNA damage reduction · Antiproliferative activity in multiple cancer cell lines · Formal development not pursued

egyptian.source_text: Ebers Papyrus (c.1550 BCE); historical records of Giza pyramid construction
egyptian.original: [Hieratic] hedj — given to workers; used in heart prescriptions
egyptian.translation: Onion — given to pyramid workers for strength and health. The Ebers Papyrus documents onion in multiple prescriptions for heart conditions, digestive complaints, and as a general tonic. Historical record: Herodotus notes that onion, garlic, and radish were inscribed on the Great Pyramid as food given to the 100,000 workers — representing perhaps the earliest documented institutional food-as-medicine programme.
egyptian.preparation: Eaten raw or cooked; applied as poultice; juice drunk
egyptian.condition: Cardiovascular conditions, digestive complaints, general strength

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II
greek_roman.translation: Onion — drying, ulcerating the skin, sharp. The juice mixed with honey is good for those with dim sight. Mixed with water of rue and applied it helps those bitten by dogs. The juice dropped into the ears relieves ringing and pain. Applied with fig it draws out splinters. Taken with vinegar it provokes sleep. The raw kind taken in excess harms the sight.
greek_roman.preparation: Juice with honey for eyes; applied with fig; taken with vinegar for sleep
greek_roman.condition: Eye conditions, dog bites, ear pain, wound healing, sleep

modern_research.key_compound: Quercetin (highest concentration of any common vegetable), allicin, fisetin, cepaenes
modern_research.summary: Quercetin: one of the most extensively studied flavonoids — anti-inflammatory (inhibits 5-LOX and COX-2), cardiovascular protective, antiviral, potential anti-cancer properties. Onion has the highest quercetin content of any commonly eaten vegetable. Multiple studies on cardiovascular risk reduction. The Anglo-Saxon Bald's Leechbook MRSA preparation includes onion/leek — the quercetin + allicin combination produces synergistic antimicrobial effects.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Egyptian and Greek/Roman traditions both document onion as a major medicinal plant for cardiovascular conditions and infection. The pyramid workers' ration record is possibly the oldest documented institutional evidence-based medicine — prescribing onion and garlic to workers for documented health benefit. Quercetin's mechanisms now explain the traditional cardiovascular use.

plants.evidence_authority: Quercetin systematic reviews for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory effects; component of UCL MRSA preparation.

tradition_names.egyptian: Documented in Egyptian texts — sacred to Min

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II
greek_roman.translation: Lettuce — soporific, cooling. The juice of the wild kind taken in drink is similar in power to opium. Applied to the forehead with rose oil it induces sleep. It diminishes sexual desire. The cultivated kind taken before eating benefits the stomach. The wild kind more powerfully than the cultivated.
greek_roman.preparation: Wild lettuce juice drunk (soporific); cultivated eaten for digestion; applied with rose oil for sleep
greek_roman.condition: Insomnia, pain, digestive complaints, sexual excess

egyptian.source_text: Egyptian religious and agricultural texts — sacred plant of Min
egyptian.original: [Hieratic] — Min's sacred plant; documented for sexual health
egyptian.translation: Lettuce was sacred to Min, the Egyptian god of fertility and virility. The tall, upright form of romaine lettuce and its white latex were associated with fertility. Paradoxically, it was also documented as calming excessive sexual desire — consistent with Dioscorides' observation. The latex of wild lettuce contains sedative compounds.
egyptian.preparation: Eaten; latex extracted from wild forms

modern_research.key_compound: Lactucin, lactucopicrin (sesquiterpene lactones — in wild lettuce), chlorophyll
modern_research.summary: Wild lettuce (Lactuca virosa): lactucin and lactucopicrin have documented sedative and analgesic activity in animal models — directly validates traditional use as a mild opium substitute. Cultivated lettuce has much lower concentrations. Historical note: wild lettuce was called 'lettuce opium' (Lactucarium) and used as an opium substitute in the 19th century when opium was unavailable. Limited modern clinical research.
modern_research.significance: The convergence between Egyptian and Greek traditions documenting lettuce (specifically wild lettuce) for sedation is pharmacologically validated by lactucin's sedative mechanism. The distinction Dioscorides makes between wild and cultivated lettuce — noting the wild kind is more powerful — is pharmacologically accurate: wild lettuce has dramatically higher lactucin concentration.

plants.evidence_authority: Lactucin sedative activity documented in vitro and animal models; used as opium substitute historically; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · Lactucin sedative mechanism validated · Opioid crisis context makes non-opioid sedatives a research priority · No clinical trials for lactucin

plants.modern_botanical: Pinus sylvestris / P. halepensis and related species

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book I; Pliny NH
greek_roman.translation: Pitch — heals wounds, expels phlegm from the chest. Liquid pitch applied warm softens swellings and draws them to a head. Taken in drink it clears the chest and stops coughs. Pliny: pine pitch was used to preserve wine in amphorae — the antimicrobial effect of resins was empirically discovered through food preservation. Applied to teeth it relieves toothache.
greek_roman.preparation: Applied warm as poultice; taken in drink for respiratory; applied to teeth
greek_roman.condition: Wound healing, respiratory complaints, dental pain, swellings

celtic_norse.original: Pic — wið wundum, wið þa þe blodigean noldon
celtic_norse.translation: Pitch/resin — for wounds, for those whose wounds would not bleed (to draw blood). Anglo-Saxon medicine uses pine pitch for wound healing and as a drawing agent for infections. Also used to seal and preserve medical preparations.
celtic_norse.preparation: Applied warm to wounds; used as sealant
celtic_norse.condition: Wound healing, infection drawing, wound sealing

modern_research.key_compound: Alpha-pinene, beta-pinene, resin acids (abietic acid, pimaric acid), terpenes
modern_research.summary: Pine resin compounds show documented antimicrobial activity — particularly alpha-pinene against respiratory pathogens. Abietic acid: anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activity. Turpentine (distilled pine resin): once widely used medically as a counterirritant and antiseptic, still in some veterinary applications. Pine resin's role in preserving wine amphorae in antiquity demonstrates empirical discovery of antimicrobial properties through food technology.
modern_research.significance: Pine resin was used medicinally across virtually every culture with access to pine trees — making it one of the most globally convergent medicinal substances in history. The antimicrobial mechanism validates both wound-healing and respiratory applications. The food preservation discovery pathway (resin in wine) demonstrates how traditional medicine knowledge often emerged through food technology observation.

plants.evidence_authority: Alpha-pinene antimicrobial documented; turpentine historically used as topical antiseptic; no approved drug in current use.
plants.priority_reason: Global convergence (virtually every pine-having culture) · Antimicrobial mechanism validated · Respiratory application plausible · Low priority given available alternatives

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book I; Pliny NH Book XXIII
greek_roman.original: ῥοιά — ἡ ῥίζα σκωλήκων φθαρτική, ἡ φλοιὸς στυπτικός
greek_roman.translation: Pomegranate — the root destroys intestinal worms; the rind is astringent. The juice of the sour kind taken in drink is good for those with stomach pain and diarrhea. The rind boiled in wine stops fluxes of the belly. The bark of the root is given in wine against tapeworms.
greek_roman.preparation: Root bark in wine for parasites; rind decocted for diarrhea; juice drunk
greek_roman.condition: Intestinal parasites (especially tapeworm), diarrhea, digestive inflammation

tibetan.source_text: Gyushi (rGyud bzhi) — medicinal plants chapter; Men-Tsee-Khang formulary
tibetan.translation: Sevu (pomegranate) — pacifies digestive disorders; heals bile conditions. The Gyushi categorises pomegranate as a cooling medicine for heat conditions (pitta-equivalent disorders in Tibetan medicine). Used for diarrhea, digestive inflammation, and as a heart tonic. One of the primary fruits in Tibetan materia medica.
tibetan.preparation: Fruit eaten; dried rind powdered and taken with water
tibetan.condition: Digestive inflammation, bile conditions, diarrhea, heart conditions

modern_research.key_compound: Punicalagins, ellagic acid, punicalin, ellagitannins, anthocyanins
modern_research.summary: Punicalagins: the most potent antioxidants documented in any commonly eaten food — three times the antioxidant activity of red wine or green tea. Ellagic acid: documented anti-inflammatory and antiproliferative activity. Multiple clinical trials for cardiovascular conditions: significant reduction in blood pressure and LDL oxidation. Punicalagin: antimicrobial activity against oral pathogens. The antiparasitic use (root bark in wine) is consistent with ellagitannin activity against helminths.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Greek/Roman and Tibetan traditions — geographically separated by the Silk Road — both document pomegranate for the identical indication of digestive inflammation and intestinal parasites. The antiparasitic pharmacology of pomegranate rind is among the most underinvestigated in the archive given the extraordinary convergence signal and the documented potency of punicalagins.

plants.evidence_authority: Extensive clinical research for cardiovascular effects; antiparasitic activity documented; no approved drug. Pomegranate juice GRAS.
plants.priority_reason: Greek/Roman and Tibetan traditions independently documented identical antiparasitic use · Ellagitannin mechanism plausible · Cardiovascular evidence strong · Antiparasitic clinical development not pursued

egyptian.original: [Hieratic] khesbed — applied to eyes; used in compound preparations for infection
egyptian.translation: Lapis lazuli ground into powder and applied to the eyes for eye conditions. Used in compound mineral prescriptions for skin infections and general health. The Ebers Papyrus includes mineral medicines alongside plant medicines — lapis lazuli was highly valued both medicinally and as a pigment.
egyptian.preparation: Ground into fine powder; applied topically to eyes; incorporated into compound preparations
egyptian.condition: Eye conditions, skin infections, general health

tibetan.translation: Lapis lazuli (béru) — beneficial for eye diseases; beneficial for difficulties of the mind. Tibetan mineral medicine places lapis lazuli among the most important mineral medicines for eye conditions and mental disturbances. The characteristic blue colour was associated with the sky and with calming properties in Tibetan cosmology.
tibetan.preparation: Ground fine; taken internally in small quantities; applied topically

modern_research.key_compound: Lazurite (Na₈[AlSiO₄]₆(SO₄,S,Cl)₂), pyrite, calcite — trace sulphur compounds
modern_research.summary: Lapis lazuli contains trace sulphur compounds from pyrite inclusions. Some antimicrobial activity documented for sulphur compounds generally. The eye application has no obvious modern pharmacological validation — the use likely reflected the traditional doctrine of signatures (blue colour associated with sky/clarity/vision) rather than active compound pharmacology. The convergence between Egyptian and Tibetan traditions for eye use may reflect transmission of doctrine rather than independent pharmacological discovery.
modern_research.significance: Lapis lazuli is one of several mineral medicines in this archive that document the early integration of geology and medicine. The Egyptian-Tibetan convergence for eye conditions is intriguing but likely represents the global application of the doctrine of signatures (blue = eye/sky/clarity) rather than a pharmacological signal. Mineral medicines require separate evaluation criteria from plant medicines.

plants.evidence_authority: Sulphur antimicrobial activity documented generally; no specific clinical evidence for lapis lazuli.
plants.priority_reason: Convergence may reflect doctrine of signatures rather than pharmacological activity · Mineral medicine — different evaluation criteria · Low priority

plants.modern_botanical: Coptis japonica / C. trifolia / C. chinensis

north_american_indigenous.source_text: Moerman's Native American Ethnobotany Database — multiple Eastern Woodlands nations
north_american_indigenous.original: [Documented oral tradition — Iroquois, Ojibwe, Micmac, Penobscot]
north_american_indigenous.translation: Goldthread (Coptis trifolia) — roots used by multiple Eastern Woodlands nations for sore mouths and throats, digestive complaints, and as a bitter tonic. The intensely yellow root (from berberine content) was recognisable and distinctive. Iroquois: root tea for sore throat and fever. Ojibwe: root for eye infections. The bright yellow colour functioned as a natural identification marker — healers across independent nations identified the same useful compound by the same visual signal.
north_american_indigenous.preparation: Root decocted as tea; applied topically to sores
north_american_indigenous.condition: Sore throat, eye infections, digestive complaints, fever

tibetan.source_text: Gyushi (rGyud bzhi) — medicinal plants; Men-Tsee-Khang formulary
tibetan.translation: Tig ta (Coptis/gentian bitter) — heals bile disorders; beneficial for eye diseases. The Gyushi categorises tig ta as a bitter medicine for heat conditions (pitta disorders). Used for jaundice, liver conditions, eye infections, and as a general antimicrobial. Berberine-containing plants (Coptis and Berberis) occupy overlapping positions in Tibetan materia medica.
tibetan.preparation: Root powdered and taken in water; applied as eye wash
tibetan.condition: Liver/bile conditions, eye infections, digestive inflammation

modern_research.key_compound: Berberine (primary alkaloid — same as goldenseal), coptisine, palmatine
modern_research.summary: Berberine — the primary alkaloid — is one of the most extensively studied plant compounds. Documented antimicrobial (including MRSA-active), antifungal, antiparasitic. Multiple clinical trials: berberine comparable to metformin for Type 2 diabetes blood glucose management. Anti-inflammatory: inhibits NF-κB pathway. Neurological: emerging evidence for Alzheimer's disease (inhibits acetylcholinesterase). The yellow colour of berberine-containing roots allowed independent cultures to identify the same pharmacologically active compound.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: North American (Coptis trifolia) and Tibetan (Coptis japonica) traditions independently documented different Coptis species for infection and digestive complaints — the same genus on opposite sides of the globe, identified for the same uses. Berberine is also the active compound in North American goldenseal (MMA-042) and Ayurvedic/Chinese Berberis. Four independent traditions discovered the same compound in related plants — the most dramatic example of parallel pharmacological discovery in the archive.

plants.evidence_authority: Cochrane and systematic reviews on berberine for Type 2 diabetes — positive results. Multiple RCTs. Berberine widely sold as supplement.
plants.priority_reason: Four independent traditions discovered berberine across Coptis, Berberis, and Hydrastis species · Diabetes evidence comparable to metformin · Antibiotic resistance application · Full clinical development not pursued

tradition_names.celtic_norse: Documented in Anglo-Saxon herbals as Pyrethrum/Pellitory

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book III
greek_roman.translation: Pellitory — beneficial for toothache. The root chewed moves saliva and warms the mouth. Applied to the face it is good for those with facial paralysis. Taken in drink with salt and oil it helps those with pain of the kidneys. The burn sensation in the mouth is characteristic of the plant.
greek_roman.preparation: Root chewed; applied topically to face; taken in drink
greek_roman.condition: Toothache, facial paralysis, kidney pain, digestive warming

celtic_norse.original: Pyrethrum — wið toðece, wið heafodece
celtic_norse.translation: Pellitory — for toothache, for headache. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents pellitory for toothache consistent with Greek/Roman documentation — one of the standard dental remedies in both traditions.
celtic_norse.preparation: Root chewed; applied to teeth

modern_research.summary: Pellitorine: alkamide compound similar to those in Echinacea and Spilanthes — produces numbing/tingling sensation in the mouth. This directly validates the traditional toothache application — the numbing effect of pellitorine provides temporary dental pain relief through TRPV1/TRPA1 receptor activation. Limited clinical research but mechanism well-understood.
modern_research.significance: Pellitory's dental application across two traditions is pharmacologically validated by the alkamide numbing mechanism — the same class of compounds responsible for the 'toothache plant' (Spilanthes) used across Africa and Asia. The convergence of a specific dental application between Greek/Roman and Anglo-Saxon traditions is consistent with the documented mechanism.

plants.evidence_authority: Pellitorine numbness mechanism documented; alkamide pharmacology studied; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · Alkamide numbing mechanism validated · Dental application consistent · Low priority

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II
greek_roman.translation: Dock — laxative, sour, diuretic. The root boiled in vinegar and drunk helps those with intestinal worms. Applied to the face it removes pimples and spots. The seed taken in drink stops diarrhea (paradox: root laxative, seed astringent). The root applied with vinegar helps those with skin conditions.
greek_roman.preparation: Root boiled in vinegar; applied topically; seed in drink
greek_roman.condition: Constipation, intestinal parasites, skin conditions, diarrhea (seed)

celtic_norse.source_text: Anglo-Saxon herbals — dock leaf famously used for nettle stings
celtic_norse.original: Docce — wið þæt man mid netle gebyrned beo, þonne genim docce leaf
celtic_norse.translation: Dock — for when one is burned by nettle, then take dock leaf. The famous folk remedy for nettle stings — applying dock leaf to reduce the burning and itching — is documented in Anglo-Saxon medicine. Also used for skin conditions and digestive complaints.
celtic_norse.preparation: Fresh leaf applied topically for nettle stings; root decocted
celtic_norse.condition: Nettle sting relief, skin conditions, digestive complaints

modern_research.key_compound: Anthraquinone glycosides (emodin, chrysophanol), tannins, oxalic acid
modern_research.summary: Emodin: laxative activity (same mechanism as senna and rhubarb anthraquinones) — validates traditional laxative use. Tannins: astringent activity — validates traditional use of seeds for diarrhea (opposite to root's laxative effect). The dock leaf nettle sting folk remedy: mechanistic explanation debated — the cooling effect of the moist leaf may be more important than specific compounds. Oxalic acid: can form kidney stones at high doses. Limited clinical research on dock specifically.
modern_research.significance: Dock represents a fascinating case where the same plant's root and seed have opposite therapeutic effects — an empirical observation made by Greek/Roman physicians and consistent with the anthraquinone (laxative, in root) vs tannin (astringent, in seed) pharmacology. The Anglo-Saxon nettle sting remedy is one of the oldest documented and most widely practised folk remedies in Northern Europe.

plants.evidence_authority: Emodin laxative mechanism documented; anthraquinone pharmacology well-studied; ESCOP monograph; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · Anthraquinone mechanism validated · Opposite root/seed effects pharmacologically explained · Low priority relative to other plants in archive

corpus_segments.label: cockayne_v2::9.  A  wound  salve :  pound  very  thoroughly,  g
corpus_segments.title: Anglo-Saxon Wound Salves and Wound Drinks (Sections 9–10)

corpus_segments.label: pliny_natural_history::chunk_0150
corpus_segments.title: Medicinal Uses of Garlic, Lettuce, and Related Plants (Pliny, Natural History, B

plants.priority_reason: Mentioned 2x across 2 tradition(s) in primary source corpus — awaiting full curation
plants.stub_note: Extracted from 2 tradition corpus file(s). 2 segment mentions. Awaiting botanical identification, cross-tradition analysis, and modern research review.

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book IV
greek_roman.translation: Broom — causes nausea, emetic. The seed taken in drink provokes vomiting. Applied as a fomentation it helps those with pain of the spleen. The flowers dried and taken help those with pain of the kidneys. Known for its purgative properties in large doses.
greek_roman.preparation: Seed in drink (emetic); flowers dried for kidney complaints; applied as fomentation
greek_roman.condition: Emetic (purging), spleen pain, kidney complaints

celtic_norse.original: Brom — wið milte sare, wið dropan
celtic_norse.translation: Broom — for spleen pain, for dropsy (edema). Anglo-Saxon medicine documents broom for oedema and spleen conditions, consistent with Greek/Roman documentation.
celtic_norse.preparation: Decocted in water

modern_research.summary: Sparteine: potent cardiac antiarrhythmic and oxytocic alkaloid — directly affects cardiac conduction. Historically used as an antiarrhythmic drug (sparteine sulfate) before being replaced by safer alternatives. Cytisine: partial nicotinic agonist — currently in clinical trials as a smoking cessation aid (less expensive than varenicline/Chantix). Traditional emetic/diuretic use is consistent with sparteine's cardiovascular effects. CAUTION: toxic in high doses — can cause cardiac arrest.
modern_research.significance: Broom's active compound cytisine is currently in clinical trials for smoking cessation — representing an active research pathway from traditional plant to modern pharmaceutical. Sparteine's cardiac activity directly validates the traditional use for dropsy (edema from heart failure). The convergence between Greek/Roman and Anglo-Saxon traditions for cardiac-related edema is pharmacologically consistent.

plants.evidence_authority: Cytisine in Phase 3 trials for smoking cessation; sparteine historically approved but now withdrawn; cardiac activity well-documented.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · Cytisine in active clinical trials for smoking cessation · Cardiac mechanism validated · Active pharmaceutical development pathway exists

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book III
greek_roman.translation: Centaury — thinning, warming, cleanses wounds. The decoction taken in drink clears the eyesight and is good for those with liver conditions. With honey it clears the complexion. Drunk in wine it helps those with sciatica. Applied to wounds it draws together the lips and cleanses foul ulcers. Named for the centaur Chiron who was said to have used it to heal Achilles' wounds — the mythological origin of the herb-wound healing tradition.
greek_roman.preparation: Decocted in wine; applied topically with honey
greek_roman.condition: Liver conditions, eye conditions, wound healing, sciatica

celtic_norse.original: Felterre — wið fefor, wið heortece
celtic_norse.translation: Centaury (felterre) — for fever, for heart conditions. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents centaury for fever and cardiac conditions, consistent with its bitter tonic properties documented in the Greek/Roman tradition.
celtic_norse.preparation: Decocted in water or ale

modern_research.summary: Swertiamarin and gentiopicroside: intensely bitter compounds that stimulate bile production and digestive enzyme secretion — pharmacological basis for traditional digestive tonic use. Documented antipyretic activity (fever-reducing) — validates traditional use for fever. Antimicrobial activity documented against several pathogens. Centaury is included in the European Pharmacopoeia as a licensed herbal medicine for digestive complaints.
modern_research.significance: Centaury's inclusion in the European Pharmacopoeia represents institutional validation of its traditional use — one of approximately 70 European plants with formal regulatory recognition. The convergence between Greek/Roman and Anglo-Saxon traditions for fever and digestive use is consistent with the documented bitter-tonic and antipyretic mechanisms.

plants.evidence_authority: European Pharmacopoeia monograph; ESCOP monograph for digestive complaints; bitter tonic mechanism validated; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · European Pharmacopoeia recognition · Bitter tonic mechanism validated · Formal drug development not pursued

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book III
greek_roman.translation: Hyssop — good for coughs; purging. Boiled with figs and honey and drunk it is good for those with chronic coughs and shortness of breath. Taken in decoction with rue it helps those with colic. The herb used as a fomentation with vinegar helps those with spleen conditions.
greek_roman.preparation: Boiled with figs and honey; decocted with rue; used as fomentation
greek_roman.condition: Chronic cough, asthma, digestive complaints, spleen conditions

celtic_norse.source_text: Anglo-Saxon herbals referencing the classical tradition
celtic_norse.original: Ysope — wið þæt man heardlice hwiste, wið breoste sare
celtic_norse.translation: Hyssop — for those who cough severely, for chest pain. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents hyssop for respiratory conditions consistent with Greek/Roman documentation.
celtic_norse.preparation: Boiled in water; taken in decoction
celtic_norse.condition: Severe cough, chest pain, respiratory complaints

modern_research.summary: Pinocamphone: antispasmodic effect on bronchial smooth muscle — pharmacological basis for respiratory traditional use. Hyssop essential oil: documented antiviral activity against herpes simplex virus in vitro. Marrubiin (also in horehound): expectorant activity. Limited clinical research on hyssop specifically despite extensive traditional documentation.
modern_research.significance: Hyssop's convergence between Greek/Roman and Anglo-Saxon traditions for respiratory use is consistent with the antispasmodic bronchodilatory mechanism of pinocamphone. The antiviral activity against herpes is an interesting modern finding not predicted by traditional medicine but emerging from the antimicrobial investigation.

plants.evidence_authority: Antispasmodic and antiviral activity documented in vitro; GRAS status; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · Antispasmodic mechanism validated · Antiviral activity against herpes documented · No clinical trials

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book I; Pliny NH
greek_roman.translation: Frankincense — astringent, warming, digestive. The resin applied to wounds stops bleeding and draws them together. Taken in drink with wine it helps those who vomit blood. The smoke of frankincense is good for those who have weak sight, when applied as a fumigation. Applied with egg white it fills out the hollow areas of wounds. Pliny: frankincense was among the most valuable traded commodities in the ancient world, second only to pepper in value.
greek_roman.preparation: Resin applied to wounds; taken in wine; burned as fumigation
greek_roman.condition: Wound healing, vomiting blood, eye conditions, ulcers

celtic_norse.source_text: Anglo-Saxon herbals and Leechbooks — frankincense in compound preparations
celtic_norse.original: Recels — wið yflum, wið þa þe on weg fareð sar
celtic_norse.translation: Incense/frankincense — against evil, against pain that departs (recurrent pain). Anglo-Saxon medicine uses frankincense in compound preparations for pain and wound healing, and in ritual healing contexts. The inclusion in both therapeutic and ritual contexts reflects frankincense's universal role as both a medicine and a sacred substance.
celtic_norse.preparation: Burned; applied topically in compound preparations

modern_research.key_compound: Boswellic acids (AKBA, KBA), incensole acetate, alpha-boswellic acid
modern_research.summary: Boswellic acids: potent 5-LOX inhibitors (leukotriene synthesis inhibitors) — documented anti-inflammatory mechanism. Multiple RCTs for osteoarthritis and inflammatory bowel disease — positive results. EU approved Boswellia extract (Casperome) for brain tumours as an orphan drug. Incensole acetate: psychoactive — activates TRPV3 channel, may explain emotional/spiritual effects of burning frankincense. AKBA: documented antiproliferative activity against cancer cell lines.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Frankincense (Boswellia) is documented in the same Burseraceae family as copal/pom — the Maya/Aztec/Andean healing resin (MMA-033). Three traditions (Greek/Roman, Anglo-Saxon, and the entire Mesoamerican complex) independently identified Burseraceae resins as medicinally significant. The boswellic acid anti-inflammatory mechanism is one of the strongest validated mechanisms in the archive for an uninvestigated-for-drug-development traditional plant.

plants.evidence_authority: Systematic reviews on Boswellia for osteoarthritis and IBD — positive results. EU orphan drug approval for brain tumour adjunct therapy.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · Cochrane evidence for arthritis and IBD · EU orphan drug approval · Convergence with Burseraceae family documented in Mesoamerican traditions · Anti-cancer research ongoing

egyptian.original: [Hieratic] qadeh — used in compound preparations for digestive and blood conditions
egyptian.translation: Cassia/cinnamon documented in multiple Ebers Papyrus prescriptions for digestive complaints, blood conditions, and as a component in compound medicinal preparations. Among the most valued spice-medicines of ancient Egypt — imported from the Far East via Arabian trade routes. Used in embalming preparations as well as medicine.
egyptian.preparation: Bark decocted in water or beer; applied in oil
egyptian.condition: Digestive complaints, blood conditions, wound dressing

celtic_norse.source_text: Anglo-Saxon herbals — cinnamon traded into Northern Europe
celtic_norse.original: Canel — wið cyle, wið wambe sare, wið fefor
celtic_norse.translation: Cinnamon — for chills, for stomach pain, for fever. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents cinnamon for warming and digestive applications, imported through Mediterranean trade routes. Used in compound preparations alongside pepper and cloves.
celtic_norse.preparation: Taken in ale or wine; applied with oil

modern_research.key_compound: Cinnamaldehyde (65-80% of essential oil), coumarin (cassia), eugenol, procyanidins
modern_research.summary: Cinnamaldehyde: insulin-sensitising activity — multiple clinical trials showing significant reduction in fasting blood glucose in Type 2 diabetes. TRPA1 agonist explaining warming sensation. Antimicrobial: broad-spectrum activity including anti-biofilm effects against oral pathogens. Coumarin (in cassia specifically): anticoagulant — present at levels that may cause liver toxicity at high doses. Ceylon cinnamon (C. verum) has much lower coumarin than cassia. Multiple meta-analyses confirm blood glucose lowering effect.
modern_research.significance: CONVERGENCE: Egyptian and Anglo-Saxon traditions document cinnamon for warming and digestive conditions. The blood glucose lowering mechanism — not known to traditional medicine as 'diabetes treatment' — validates the traditional description of cinnamon as beneficial for 'thirst' and 'weakness' (classic diabetes symptoms). Cinnamon is one of the most commercially studied traditional medicines for Type 2 diabetes globally.

plants.evidence_authority: Cochrane review and multiple meta-analyses for cinnamon in Type 2 diabetes — positive results. CAUTION: cassia variety contains hepatotoxic coumarin at high doses.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · Blood glucose mechanism validated · Type 2 diabetes RCT evidence · Coumarin toxicity concern in cassia variety limits development

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II; Pliny NH
greek_roman.translation: Mallow — softening, anti-inflammatory. The leaves and root boiled and eaten or taken in decoction loosen the belly. Applied as a fomentation they soften swellings. The juice of the leaves applied to the skin removes blemishes. Mixed with oil it is good for the sting of bees and wasps. Pliny: mallow was described by both Pythagoras and Plato as among the most beneficial plants for human health.
greek_roman.preparation: Leaves boiled and eaten; applied as fomentation; juice applied topically
greek_roman.condition: Constipation, swellings, skin blemishes, insect stings, respiratory

celtic_norse.original: Mealwe — wið hrif sare, wið wunda weorðan hate
celtic_norse.translation: Mallow — for belly pain, for wounds becoming inflamed. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents mallow for digestive and wound-inflammatory applications consistent with Greek/Roman tradition.
celtic_norse.preparation: Boiled in water; applied as poultice
celtic_norse.condition: Abdominal pain, wound inflammation, digestive complaints

modern_research.summary: Mallow mucilage: soothing effect on inflamed mucous membranes — pharmacological basis for respiratory and digestive traditional use. Malvidin: antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity. Mallow is one of the primary plants in the European 'emollient' tradition — used to soothe inflammation of mucous membranes from mouth to intestine. Multiple studies confirm anti-inflammatory activity of mallow extracts. Widely used in European herbal medicine for coughs and digestive complaints.
modern_research.significance: Mallow's convergence between Greek/Roman and Anglo-Saxon traditions for soothing inflammation is pharmacologically validated by the mucilage mechanism. The plant's global use as a demulcent — soothing inflamed surfaces — across dozens of independent traditions represents one of the clearest examples of empirical pharmacological discovery: healers noticed that mallow soothes irritation, and the mucilage mechanism explains exactly why.

plants.evidence_authority: Traditional use well-documented; mucilage mechanism validated; ESCOP monograph; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: 2 traditions · Mucilage soothing mechanism well-validated · Respiratory application widely used in European herbal medicine · Full clinical development not pursued

plants.modern_botanical: Myrcianthes cisplatensis / Myrtus communis (Old World Myrtle)

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Books V–VI — most frequently mentioned Andean medicinal plant
cobo.original: El arrayán es árbol muy medicinal. Su corteza y hojas cocidas en agua sirven para lavar heridas, llagas y apostemas. Las hojas mascadas confortan las encías y aprietan los dientes flojos.
cobo.translation: The arrayán (myrtle) is a very medicinal tree. Its bark and leaves boiled in water serve to wash wounds, sores, and abscesses. The leaves chewed strengthen the gums and tighten loose teeth. Cobo documents arrayán as the single most-mentioned medicinal plant in his Andean observations — appearing in 15 separate therapeutic contexts. Used for wound washing, dental health, digestive complaints, and respiratory conditions. Indigenous Andean healers used it systematically across all these applications.
cobo.preparation: Bark and leaves decocted for wound washing; leaves chewed for dental conditions; decoction drunk for digestive complaints
cobo.condition: Wound infection, dental disease, digestive complaints, respiratory conditions
cobo.notes: Arrayán's prominence in Cobo — 15 mentions, more than any other Andean plant except cinchona — suggests it was the most versatile and widely used plant in the indigenous Andean pharmacopoeia that Cobo documented. The fact that it appears consistently across wound care, dental medicine, and digestive applications across different indigenous communities suggests genuine pharmacological value rather than localised traditional practice.

modern_research.summary: Myrtaceae family plants consistently show antimicrobial activity from essential oil components. Myricetin: documented antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity. Eugenol: the same compound responsible for clove's dental application — directly validates the traditional dental use of arrayán. Multiple studies on South American Myrtaceae species showing antimicrobial activity against wound pathogens. Cobo's wound-washing application is consistent with the antimicrobial essential oil content.
modern_research.significance: The most-documented Andean plant in Cobo's archive with 15 mentions — yet almost entirely absent from modern pharmacological research. The Myrtaceae family's antimicrobial properties are well-established globally (tea tree oil is the most commercially developed example); the South American arrayán species represent an under-investigated branch of the same family with likely similar antimicrobial mechanisms.

plants.evidence_authority: Myrtaceae essential oil antimicrobial activity well-documented; eugenol dental application validated (also in clove); no clinical trials for arrayán specifically.
plants.priority_reason: Most-mentioned plant in Cobo's entire archive · Eugenol dental mechanism validated · Wound antimicrobial application consistent with Myrtaceae pharmacology · Zero clinical research despite 15 documented uses
