greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II; Hippocratic corpus — barley gruel (ptisane)
greek_roman.original: κριθή — ἡ πτισάνη τρέφει, οὐρεῖ, λύει κοιλίαν
greek_roman.translation: Barley — the barley gruel (ptisane) nourishes, promotes urine, loosens the bowels. Hippocrates prescribed barley gruel as the primary therapeutic food for fever and acute illness — ptisane is the most frequently mentioned therapeutic preparation in the entire Hippocratic corpus. Applied as a poultice it softens and reduces swellings. The grain fermented produces a drink good for those with urinary complaints. Dioscorides: barley meal mixed with fenugreek applied as a poultice ripens abscesses.
greek_roman.preparation: Grain boiled as gruel (ptisane); applied as poultice with other plants; fermented as medicinal drink
greek_roman.condition: Fever (primary Hippocratic remedy), digestive complaints, urinary conditions, wound swellings
greek_roman.notes: Barley ptisane — barley gruel — was Hippocrates' universal fever remedy. The Hippocratic text On Regimen in Acute Diseases is essentially a treatise on when and how to administer barley gruel. This makes barley the most important single therapeutic substance in the foundation document of Western medicine.

modern_research.key_compound: Beta-glucan (7-15% of grain), tocols, phenolic acids, avenanthramides
modern_research.summary: Beta-glucan: FDA qualified health claim for cardiovascular risk reduction — lowers LDL cholesterol and blood glucose. Multiple RCTs confirming beta-glucan efficacy for Type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease. The mechanism validates the traditional observation that barley 'nourishes' during fever — beta-glucan's slow-release energy and immune-modulating properties are consistent with Hippocratic fever management. Beta-glucan also documented to stimulate immune function.
modern_research.significance: Barley is the foundation of the Western medical tradition's most important therapeutic food — Hippocratic ptisane. The beta-glucan mechanism now validates the nutritional and metabolic rationale for barley-based fever therapy. FDA has granted a qualified health claim for beta-glucan cardiovascular benefit, making barley one of the few traditional therapeutic foods with formal regulatory recognition.

plants.evidence_authority: FDA qualified health claim for barley beta-glucan and cardiovascular disease risk reduction. Beta-glucan in USP. EFSA approved health claim in EU.

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book V
cobo.original: El molle es árbol muy estimado de los indios por sus muchas virtudes medicinales. Con su resina curan heridas y apostemas. Sus hojas cocidas en agua son buenas para lavar heridas viejas y úlceras.
cobo.translation: The molle is a tree greatly esteemed by the Indians for its many medicinal virtues. With its resin they cure wounds and abscesses. Its leaves boiled in water are good for washing old wounds and ulcers. The fruit is used to make chicha de molle — a fermented drink used both as food and medicine. The bark decocted is used for toothache and as a purgative. Cobo describes molle as one of the most versatile medicinal trees in Andean medicine — comparable to the European oak in the breadth of its applications.
cobo.preparation: Resin applied to wounds; leaves decocted for wound washing; fruit fermented as medicinal chicha; bark decocted for dental pain
cobo.condition: Wound infection, dental pain, digestive complaints, respiratory conditions, as purgative
cobo.notes: Schinus molle (Peruvian pepper tree) is now globally distributed — planted as an ornamental and shade tree across Southern Europe, Africa, and California. The medicinal uses documented by Cobo for Andean communities have since been documented independently by multiple African communities who adopted the introduced tree — an interesting case of post-colonial convergence where an introduced plant's medicinal properties were independently rediscovered.

modern_research.key_compound: Alpha-phellandrene, beta-phellandrene, myrtenal, bornyl acetate, cardanol (anacardic acid in fruit)
modern_research.summary: Multiple studies confirming antimicrobial activity of Schinus molle essential oil — particularly against Staphylococcus aureus, E. coli, and Candida. Anacardic acids in the fruit show significant antimicrobial activity consistent with traditional wound-healing use. Anti-inflammatory activity documented. The plant is now studied by Brazilian and Peruvian researchers actively. Dental application: essential oil shows activity against oral pathogens consistent with traditional toothache use.
modern_research.significance: Schinus molle is one of the most pharmacologically validated Andean plants with active modern research, yet virtually absent from international pharmaceutical development pipelines. The convergence between Cobo's 17th-century documentation and modern antimicrobial research is direct and mechanistically coherent. This plant represents the archive's clearest example of an Andean medicine with genuine drug development potential that has received no serious clinical investment.

plants.evidence_authority: Multiple antimicrobial studies published in peer-reviewed journals; anti-inflammatory activity documented; no clinical trials.
plants.priority_reason: 7 mentions in Cobo · Antimicrobial activity confirmed in multiple studies · Drug-resistant pathogen activity documented · No clinical development despite strong preclinical evidence

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book V
cobo.original: El saúco es árbol muy conocido y de grande uso en la medicina del Perú. Sus flores y hojas son muy buenas para los constipados y para ablandar apostemas.
cobo.translation: The saúco (elder) is a very well-known tree of great use in Peruvian medicine. Its flowers and leaves are very good for colds and for softening abscesses. The bark decocted is used as a diuretic and purgative. The flowers applied externally reduce inflammation. Cobo notes that both Spanish colonists and indigenous Andean healers used saúco — the Andean species (S. peruviana) was found to have the same properties as the European elder (S. nigra) that Spanish physicians already knew, creating one of the earliest documented cross-continental pharmacological comparisons.
cobo.preparation: Flowers decocted for respiratory complaints; leaves applied externally for inflammation; bark decocted as diuretic
cobo.condition: Colds, respiratory infection, fever, inflammatory swellings, diuretic

modern_research.key_compound: Sambucol (standardised elderberry extract), anthocyanins, sambunigrin, quercetin
modern_research.summary: European elderberry (S. nigra) has multiple clinical trials showing reduction in cold and flu duration. Sambucol (standardised extract): RCT showing 50% reduction in flu duration. Antiviral activity: anthocyanins bind and prevent viral entry into cells. Multiple meta-analyses confirm efficacy for upper respiratory infections. The South American species (S. peruviana) is less studied but likely shares the same active compounds given botanical proximity.
modern_research.significance: Elderberry/elder is one of the most clinically validated traditional plants for respiratory infections in the archive — with RCT evidence for flu reduction that predates modern antiviral drugs. Cobo's documentation of the Andean elder for the same indication as European elder represents an early cross-continental botanical comparison. The convergence between European folk medicine and Andean traditional use — for the same plant genus, same indication — strengthens the pharmacological signal.

plants.evidence_authority: Systematic review on elderberry for respiratory infections — positive results. Multiple RCTs. Sambucol commercially available.

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book VI
cobo.original: La zábila es planta muy conocida. El acíbar, que es el jugo que se saca de sus hojas, es muy amargo y purga la cólera. Aplicado a heridas y quemaduras es de grande provecho.
cobo.translation: The zábila (aloe) is a very well-known plant. The acíbar, which is the juice extracted from its leaves, is very bitter and purges bile (acts as a laxative). Applied to wounds and burns it is of great benefit. Cobo documents both the internal (laxative) and external (wound/burn) applications that remain the two primary modern uses of aloe — a remarkable pharmacological accuracy across four centuries.
cobo.preparation: Gel applied directly to wounds and burns; bitter latex (acíbar) taken as laxative
cobo.notes: The distinction Cobo makes between 'acíbar' (the bitter yellow latex — laxative) and the gel (wound healing) is pharmacologically precise. The latex contains anthraquinone glycosides (laxative); the inner gel contains polysaccharides and glycoproteins (wound healing). Cobo identified both components and their different applications in the 17th century — predating the chemical understanding by 300 years.

modern_research.key_compound: Aloin (anthraquinone glycoside — in latex), acemannan (polysaccharide — in gel), glucomannans, salicylic acid
modern_research.summary: Aloe vera gel: FDA cleared for wound care; documented wound healing acceleration and burn treatment. Aloe latex: FDA previously approved OTC laxative until 2002 when data gaps led to reclassification pending studies. Acemannan: immunomodulatory activity. Multiple RCTs for wound healing, burn treatment, and psoriasis. One of the most studied traditional plants globally.
modern_research.significance: Aloe vera is one of the most commercially successful traditional medicines globally — a multi-billion dollar industry. Cobo's 17th-century documentation of both its laxative and wound-healing applications matches precisely the two validated modern uses, demonstrating that the traditional observation was pharmacologically accurate.

plants.evidence_authority: Aloe vera gel: FDA cleared wound care device (510k). Aloe latex: formerly FDA approved OTC laxative (now reclassified pending data). USP monograph.

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II
greek_roman.translation: Bean — produces gas, diuretic. Applied as a poultice the raw meal reduces swellings. Applied with vinegar it removes spots from the face. Taken in drink with goose fat it eases the pains of the kidneys and bladder. Boiled and eaten with salt it causes dreaming.
greek_roman.preparation: Meal applied as poultice; taken in drink; eaten cooked
greek_roman.condition: Urinary complaints, kidney pain, skin spots, digestive gas

modern_research.key_compound: Soluble fibre (pectin), resistant starch, protein, lectins, polyphenols
modern_research.summary: Common bean: extensive evidence for cardiovascular benefit — lowers LDL cholesterol, blood glucose regulation. Multiple meta-analyses on legume consumption. High protein content validates nutritional documentation. Lectins: antimicrobial activity but potentially toxic if raw. Resistant starch: documented prebiotic effect.
modern_research.significance: The Greek/Roman bean documentation for urinary and kidney complaints, while not specifically validated for those indications, is consistent with the diuretic effect of legume consumption. The broader cardiovascular evidence base for legume consumption validates the general therapeutic use of beans.

plants.evidence_authority: Cardiovascular meta-analyses positive for legume consumption; resistant starch prebiotic documented; lectins potentially toxic raw; no approved drug.

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II; Pliny NH Book XVIII
greek_roman.translation: Rape/turnip — promotes seed (sperm) production, laxative. Dioscorides: applied with honey it removes freckles and spots. Taken with honey and vinegar it eases splenic conditions. The seed pressed for oil is a lamp oil, used medicinally for skin conditions. Pliny documents turnip as one of the most important Roman agricultural and medicinal plants.
greek_roman.preparation: Root eaten; oil pressed from seeds for topical use; applied with honey
greek_roman.condition: Digestive complaints, skin conditions, spleen conditions, general nutrition

modern_research.key_compound: Glucosinolates (progoitrin, gluconapin), erucic acid (seed oil), sinigrin, isothiocyanates
modern_research.summary: Glucosinolates yield isothiocyanates — same cancer-preventive Brassica compounds. Sinigrin: antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activity. Erucic acid in rapeseed oil: at high concentrations associated with cardiac toxicity; modern canola oil bred to have low erucic acid. Glucosinolates in Brassica are among the most active dietary cancer-preventive compounds.
modern_research.significance: Turnip/rape belongs to the same Brassica family as broccoli, watercress, and rocket — all sharing the glucosinolate cancer-preventive compound class. Pliny's extensive documentation of turnip as important medicine reflects its status as a staple food-medicine.

plants.evidence_authority: Glucosinolate pharmacology documented; isothiocyanate cancer prevention ongoing; canola oil (low erucic acid variety) widely used; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: Glucosinolates connect to active Brassica cancer research · Sinigrin antimicrobial documented · Food-medicine continuum

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book III; Pliny NH
greek_roman.translation: Basil — benefits the stomach, is diuretic, promotes milk production. Dioscorides: taken in drink it eases pain of the stomach. Applied with wine and oil it eases headache. The seed taken in drink moves the menstrual flux. Dried and taken as a snuff it clears the head. Pliny records a striking paradox: some ancient authorities consider basil harmful and others consider it highly beneficial — one of the first documented disagreements in Western medical literature about a plant's properties.
greek_roman.preparation: Taken in drink; applied with wine and oil; seed in drink; dried as snuff
greek_roman.condition: Digestive complaints, headache, menstrual disorders, lactation, respiratory congestion

modern_research.key_compound: Eugenol (in O. basilicum), ursolic acid, rosmarinic acid, linalool, eugenol (O. sanctum: highest concentration)
modern_research.summary: Holy basil (O. sanctum / Tulsi): the most extensively studied basil species. Multiple RCTs showing adaptogenic effect — significant reduction in stress markers, anxiety, and cognitive fatigue. Anti-diabetic: documented blood glucose reduction. Antimicrobial: eugenol (high in both species) shows broad-spectrum activity. O. sanctum included in Ayurvedic medicine as a sacred adaptogen — now backed by clinical evidence. Western basil (O. basilicum): rosmarinic acid and linalool show anti-anxiety and anti-inflammatory effects.
modern_research.significance: Basil occupies a fascinating position across traditions — valued medicinally in Greek/Roman medicine, sacred in Indian tradition (Tulsi), and now validated clinically primarily through the Ayurvedic research pathway on Holy Basil. The Greek/Roman documentation for digestive and anxiety-related conditions is consistent with the documented mechanisms, though the research has developed primarily through the Indian rather than the Western tradition.

plants.evidence_authority: Systematic reviews on Tulsi/Holy Basil for stress and metabolic conditions — positive results. Multiple RCTs.

egyptian.original: [Hieratic] dab — used in poultices and compound prescriptions for wounds and digestive conditions
egyptian.translation: Figs appear in multiple Ebers Papyrus prescriptions as a component in compound medicines — combined with other plants for wound poultices, for digestive complaints, and for chest conditions. Used both internally and externally. The ripe fig applied as a poultice was documented for infected swellings. Fig juice mixed with other ingredients for internal complaints. Among the most commonly used plant foods in Egyptian medicine.
egyptian.preparation: Fresh fig applied as poultice; fig juice mixed in compound preparations; dried fig decocted
egyptian.condition: Wound infection, digestive complaints, respiratory conditions, constipation

modern_research.key_compound: Ficin (proteolytic enzyme), psoralen (furanocoumarin), quercetin, anthocyanins, digestive enzymes
modern_research.summary: Ficin: proteolytic enzyme similar to papain — breaks down protein, explains wound-debriding and digestive properties. Psoralen: furocoumarin that sensitises skin to UV — used medicinally for psoriasis and vitiligo (PUVA therapy). Anthocyanins: anti-inflammatory, antioxidant. Multiple studies on fig extract for digestive conditions — positive results for constipation. The poultice application for infected wounds is consistent with ficin's protein-digesting activity removing necrotic tissue.
modern_research.significance: Fig's use as a wound poultice in Egyptian medicine is pharmacologically validated by ficin's proteolytic activity — the same mechanism as papaya-derived papain, now used in medical wound debridement products. The connection between the oldest documented wound-healing fruit and modern enzymatic debridement is direct.

plants.evidence_authority: Ficin proteolytic activity documented; psoralen used in PUVA therapy (FDA approved for psoriasis); fig extract digestive studies positive; no approved drug specifically from fig.
plants.priority_reason: Ficin wound debridement mechanism validated · Psoralen PUVA therapy connection · Digestive enzyme activity consistent with traditional use

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book VI
cobo.original: El romero es planta muy estimada en la medicina española y criolla del Perú por sus muchas virtudes.
cobo.translation: Rosemary is a plant highly esteemed in Spanish and Creole medicine in Peru for its many virtues. Used for head conditions, to strengthen the memory, for digestive complaints, and as a general tonic. Cobo documents that rosemary was among the first European plants successfully cultivated in Andean Peru and rapidly adopted by indigenous healers, who found its properties consistent with local aromatic plants they already used.
cobo.preparation: Decocted in wine or water for internal use; applied as compress; burned as aromatic fumigation
cobo.condition: Memory and cognitive conditions, headache, digestive complaints, general tonic

modern_research.key_compound: Rosmarinic acid, carnosic acid, ursolic acid, 1,8-cineole, camphor
modern_research.summary: Rosmarinic acid: documented anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antiviral activity. Carnosic acid: strong antioxidant; neuroprotective activity in animal models. Multiple studies on cognitive enhancement: rosmarinic acid inhibits acetylcholinesterase (same mechanism as Alzheimer's drugs). Small clinical trials showing improved memory performance with rosemary aroma. 1,8-cineole correlates with cognitive performance in human studies.
modern_research.significance: Rosemary's traditional use for memory — documented by Cobo and ubiquitous in European folk medicine ('rosemary for remembrance') — is now supported by mechanistic research showing acetylcholinesterase inhibition, the same target as approved Alzheimer's drugs. The gap between traditional folk observation and potential therapeutic application for cognitive decline is one of the most clinically significant in the archive.

plants.evidence_authority: Rosmarinic acid pharmacology documented; cognitive studies positive; GRAS status; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: Traditional memory use validated by acetylcholinesterase inhibition · Alzheimer's drug target · Small clinical trials positive · Full development not pursued

celtic_norse.original: Merce — wið blæddre sare, wið wambe sare, wið cyle
celtic_norse.translation: Alexanders — for bladder pain, for stomach pain, for chills. Bald's Leechbook documents alexanders extensively for urinary and digestive conditions. A major vegetable and medicine in Roman Britain and Anglo-Saxon England before celery displaced it — sometimes called 'the poor man's lovage.' Used as both a food vegetable and medicine for the same range of conditions.
celtic_norse.preparation: Stems and leaves eaten as vegetable; decocted for internal conditions
celtic_norse.condition: Bladder conditions, digestive complaints, chills, menstrual disorders

modern_research.summary: Very limited modern research. Phthalides (also in celery) — antispasmodic and diuretic activity documented for the celery phthalide class. The displacement of alexanders by celery in European food culture may have inadvertently continued the same therapeutic tradition, as both plants share similar phytochemical profiles.
modern_research.significance: Alexanders represents a plant that fell out of medical use not because of inefficacy but because of cultural displacement by celery — a fascinating case of therapeutic continuity through botanical substitution. The limited modern research means this plant is genuinely unknown territory.

plants.evidence_authority: Phthalide pharmacology documented in related Apiaceae; no research specific to Smyrnium olusatrum.
plants.priority_reason: Single tradition · Displaced by celery · Limited research · Lower priority relative to other archive plants

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book II; Pliny NH
greek_roman.translation: Rocket — aphrodisiac, promotes sperm production. Dioscorides: taken with vinegar it helps the spleen. Applied with vinegar it removes freckles and blemishes. The seed is warming. Pliny documents rocket as one of the most popular aphrodisiac plants of ancient Rome — mixed with pine nuts and pepper. The aphrodisiac reputation was consistent enough that rocket was prohibited from being planted near shrines of Adonis.
greek_roman.preparation: Eaten raw; seed taken in drink; applied with vinegar topically
greek_roman.condition: Digestive complaints, fertility, skin blemishes, spleen conditions

modern_research.key_compound: Glucosinolates (glucosativin, glucoerucin), erucic acid, isothiocyanates, nitrates
modern_research.summary: Glucosinolates yield isothiocyanates — same cancer-preventive compounds as in watercress and broccoli. Nitrates (high in arugula): converted to nitric oxide, vasodilatory — possibly the basis for traditional 'aphrodisiac' reputation (improved blood flow). Multiple epidemiological studies connect arugula consumption to cardiovascular benefit. The Brassica family's cancer-preventive evidence base broadly applies.
modern_research.significance: Rocket's ancient reputation as an aphrodisiac may have a plausible pharmacological basis — nitrate-derived nitric oxide improves vascular function and blood flow through the same mechanism as sildenafil (Viagra), though far less potently. This connection between ancient observation and modern pharmacology is one of the more entertaining convergences in the archive.

plants.evidence_authority: Nitrate vasodilatory mechanism documented; isothiocyanate cancer prevention research ongoing; GRAS; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: Nitrate → nitric oxide mechanism may validate traditional aphrodisiac use · Isothiocyanate cancer prevention research ongoing

greek_roman.source_text: De Materia Medica, Dioscorides, Book III; Pliny NH
greek_roman.translation: Parsley — diuretic, moves the menstrual flux. Dioscorides: the seed and root taken in drink provoke urine. The seed moves the menstrual flux and expels the afterbirth. Taken with wine it helps those with pain of the kidneys and bladder. Applied to the eyes it reduces inflammation. Among Roman physicians, parsley seed was a primary medicine for urinary and gynecological complaints.
greek_roman.preparation: Seed and root in drink; applied to eyes; taken in wine for kidney conditions
greek_roman.condition: Urinary complaints, menstrual disorders, kidney conditions, eye inflammation

modern_research.key_compound: Apiol (essential oil — uterotonic), myristicin, flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin), vitamin K
modern_research.summary: Apiol: documented uterotonic activity — validates traditional use as emmenagogue and for menstrual regulation. CAUTION: apiol is toxic at high doses — parsley seed oil was historically used as an abortifacient with documented fatalities. Diuretic activity: consistent with traditional use for urinary conditions. Luteolin: anti-inflammatory and antioxidant. Vitamin K: highest concentration of any common herb — relevant for anticoagulant interactions. Myristicin: mild psychoactive properties at high doses.
modern_research.significance: Parsley's traditional use as a menstrual regulator is pharmacologically validated by apiol's uterotonic activity — the same mechanism explains both its therapeutic use for delayed menstruation and its historical use as an abortifacient. This is one of the clearest cases in the archive of traditional medicine correctly identifying a uterotonic mechanism centuries before the pharmacology was understood. CAUTION: potentially dangerous in pregnancy.

plants.evidence_authority: Apiol uterotonic activity documented; vitamin K content well-established; diuretic activity confirmed; no approved drug (historical abortifacient use was dangerous).
plants.priority_reason: Uterotonic mechanism validated · Historical applications span legitimate and dangerous uses · Luteolin anti-inflammatory research ongoing

tradition_names.egyptian: Documented in Ebers Papyrus compound prescriptions

egyptian.original: [Hieratic] — caraway seed in digestive compound prescriptions
egyptian.translation: Caraway seed documented in the Ebers Papyrus as a component in compound medicines for digestive complaints — used for flatulence, abdominal pain, and as a digestive stimulant. Among the oldest documented culinary-medicinal plants, with evidence of use in ancient Egypt dating to at least 1550 BCE and archaeological finds suggesting use as far back as 5000 BCE.
egyptian.preparation: Seeds decocted or chewed; incorporated into compound preparations

modern_research.key_compound: Carvone (50-85% of essential oil), limonene, carveol
modern_research.summary: Carvone (same compound as in dill): antispasmodic activity on gastrointestinal smooth muscle — directly validates traditional digestive use. European Commission E monograph approves caraway for dyspeptic complaints. Multiple clinical studies confirming efficacy for functional dyspepsia and irritable bowel symptoms. One of the most clearly validated traditional digestive herbs in European regulatory frameworks.
modern_research.significance: Caraway has one of the clearest regulatory validations among traditional digestive plants — Commission E approval, ESCOP monograph, and documented antispasmodic mechanism. The 5,000-year documented use (archaeological) combined with modern regulatory recognition makes it one of the most time-tested validated plant medicines in the archive.

plants.evidence_authority: EU Commission E approved for dyspeptic complaints; ESCOP monograph; clinical evidence for functional dyspepsia.

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book IV
cobo.original: La papa es el principal sustento de los indios del Perú. Además de servir de comida, tiene uso medicinal: cocida y aplicada en cataplasma sirve para calmar el ardor y la inflamación.
cobo.translation: The potato is the principal sustenance of the Indians of Peru. Besides serving as food, it has medicinal use: cooked and applied as a poultice it serves to calm burning and inflammation. Cobo is among the first European writers to systematically document the potato's medicinal as well as nutritional role. Indigenous Andean communities distinguished between dozens of potato varieties with different medicinal properties — a pharmacological knowledge system of extraordinary sophistication.
cobo.preparation: Cooked and applied as poultice for inflammation; eaten for nutritional conditions; raw juice applied topically
cobo.condition: Inflammatory conditions (topical), nutritional deficiencies, digestive complaints

modern_research.key_compound: Solanine (in skin/green parts — toxic), chlorogenic acid, resistant starch, vitamin C, potassium
modern_research.summary: Potato skin: solanine (toxic at high doses) and chlorogenic acid (anti-inflammatory, antioxidant). Resistant starch in cooled cooked potato: documented prebiotic effect. Raw potato juice: anti-ulcer activity documented in small trials (alkaline pH buffers gastric acid; anti-inflammatory compounds). Topical use validated by anti-inflammatory compound profile. The enormous genetic diversity of Andean potatoes (over 3,000 varieties) represents an underexplored pharmacogenomic resource.
modern_research.significance: Cobo's documentation of the potato's dual food-medicine role captures one of the most important Andean contributions to global nutrition. The medicinal applications — particularly the topical anti-inflammatory use and the raw juice gastric protection — are consistent with the documented chemistry. The 3,000+ Andean potato varieties represent a genetic library of Solanaceae diversity that modern pharmacology has barely begun to explore.

plants.evidence_authority: Resistant starch prebiotic activity documented; chlorogenic acid pharmacology studied; raw potato juice anti-ulcer pilot studies; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: 3,000+ Andean varieties represent unexplored pharmacological diversity · Chlorogenic acid anti-inflammatory documented · Solanaceae family connections to important alkaloid pharmacology

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book V
cobo.original: La muña es hierba muy olorosa que los indios usan mucho para los males del estómago y para conservar las papas.
cobo.translation: Muña is a very fragrant herb that the Indians use greatly for stomach ailments and for preserving potatoes. Cobo documents two distinct uses: medicinal (digestive complaints, respiratory conditions) and food preservation — the practice of storing potatoes with muña to prevent insect damage and spoilage. This dual use as food preservative and medicine is consistent with the documented antimicrobial properties.
cobo.preparation: Leaves chewed or decocted for digestive complaints; used as fumigation for respiratory; placed with stored food as preservative
cobo.condition: Digestive complaints, respiratory conditions, altitude sickness (soroche), food preservation
cobo.notes: Muña is one of the few plants in the archive documented simultaneously as medicine and food preservative — a dual use that reflects a sophisticated empirical understanding of its antimicrobial properties. Modern research has validated both applications.

modern_research.summary: Muña essential oil shows potent antimicrobial activity — particularly against food spoilage organisms, validating the traditional potato preservation use. Carvacrol and thymol (also in oregano and thyme) show broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity including against drug-resistant pathogens. Anti-parasitic: documented activity against Giardia in vitro. Altitude sickness (soroche): muña is traditionally used by Andean communities for altitude adaptation; limited pharmacological research on this specific application.
modern_research.significance: Muña's dual documentation as medicine and food preservative in Cobo is one of the clearest examples of empirical antimicrobial discovery in traditional practice — healers identified the same plant as useful for preventing food spoilage and treating internal infections, both consistent with the documented carvacrol/thymol antimicrobial mechanism.

plants.evidence_authority: Antimicrobial activity documented in multiple Peruvian studies; carvacrol/thymol pharmacology well-established; no clinical trials.
plants.priority_reason: Dual food-preservative and medicinal use consistent with antimicrobial mechanism · Drug-resistant pathogen activity · Active research in Peru · No international clinical development

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book VI
cobo.original: Las malvas son de grande uso en la medicina española y criolla del Perú.
cobo.translation: Mallows are of great use in Spanish and Creole medicine in Peru. Cobo documents mallow for digestive complaints, respiratory conditions, and wound treatment — extending the existing Greek/Roman and Anglo-Saxon documentation for mallow (MMA-077) to a third tradition via adoption in colonial Peru.
cobo.condition: Digestive complaints, respiratory conditions, wound healing, inflammation

modern_research.summary: See MMA-077 for full pharmacological summary. This Cobo entry adds a third independent documentation tradition for mallow — Spanish colonial Peru adopting European mallow for the same applications, demonstrating the plant's consistent therapeutic recognition across cultures.
modern_research.significance: Extends mallow convergence (MMA-077) to three independent documentation contexts: Greek/Roman, Anglo-Saxon, and colonial Andean. Post-colonial adoption by Andean healers represents independent empirical validation of European mallow medicine.

plants.evidence_authority: See MMA-077. Mucilage soothing mechanism validated; ESCOP monograph; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: Extends mallow to three documentation traditions including independent Andean adoption · Mucilage mechanism validated

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book V
cobo.original: La cidra es fruta sana y medicinal. Su corteza confitada es buena para el estómago.
cobo.translation: The citron is a healthy and medicinal fruit. Its candied rind is good for the stomach and for those with corruption in the mouth (oral infection). Cobo documents citron for digestive complaints, oral infection, and as a general health tonic — consistent with its role as the oldest cultivated citrus in ancient Mediterranean medicine.
cobo.preparation: Rind candied or decocted; juice drunk; applied topically
cobo.condition: Digestive complaints, oral infection, skin conditions, general tonic

modern_research.summary: Citron is the oldest cultivated citrus — ancestor of all other citrus species. Diosmin (citrus flavonoid derivative): approved pharmaceutical drug in France for venous insufficiency. Hesperidin: capillary-strengthening, anti-inflammatory. Limonene: antimicrobial, antifungal.
modern_research.significance: Citron's position as the ancestor of all modern citrus gives it particular historical significance. Diosmin derived from citrus flavonoids is an approved European pharmaceutical, making citrus one of the most commercially developed traditional medicine plant families.

plants.evidence_authority: Diosmin (citrus flavonoid derivative) approved as drug in France for venous insufficiency; hesperidin pharmacology documented.

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book VI
cobo.original: La clavellina fue traída de España y es apreciada por sus virtudes medicinales.
cobo.translation: The carnation was brought from Spain and is appreciated for its medicinal virtues. Cobo documents carnation flower for heart conditions, as a cardiac tonic, for fevers, and as a nervine. Also used as a flavoring in medicinal preparations. Introduced by Spanish colonists but adopted by Andean healers.
cobo.preparation: Flowers decocted; petals infused in wine
cobo.condition: Heart conditions, fever, digestive complaints, nervine

modern_research.key_compound: Eugenol (clove-like compound in flowers), flavonoids, saponins
modern_research.summary: Carnation contains eugenol — the same compound responsible for clove's antimicrobial and analgesic properties. Traditional cardiac tonic use not well-studied pharmacologically. Flavonoids with anti-inflammatory activity. Limited modern research on Dianthus specifically.
modern_research.significance: Carnation's eugenol content connects it to clove's validated pharmacology for pain and antimicrobial applications. The cardiac tonic use documented by Cobo is pharmacologically unstudied but plausible given eugenol's documented cardiovascular effects.

plants.evidence_authority: Eugenol pharmacology documented (primarily in cloves); limited research on Dianthus specifically.
plants.priority_reason: Eugenol connects to validated clove pharmacology · Cardiac use unstudied · Low priority relative to other archive plants

cobo.source_text: Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Cobo (1653), Book IV
cobo.original: La chácara es tubérculo muy estimado de los indios del Perú después de la papa.
cobo.translation: The chácara (oca) is a tuber highly esteemed by the Indians of Peru after the potato. Cobo documents oca as a food-medicine — the sour varieties used for digestive conditions, the sweet varieties as a general food. Used medicinally for kidney complaints, urinary conditions, and digestive complaints. The high oxalic acid content explains the sour taste and traditional use for acidic digestive conditions.
cobo.preparation: Tubers eaten cooked or freeze-dried (chuno method); applied as poultice
cobo.condition: Digestive complaints, kidney conditions, general nutrition

modern_research.key_compound: Oxalic acid, anthocyanins (in red varieties), vitamin C, starch
modern_research.summary: Oca is the second most important Andean tuber after potato. Oxalic acid: explains sour taste; at high concentrations can cause kidney stones. Anthocyanins in red oca: anti-inflammatory and antioxidant. Vitamin C: high content. The freeze-drying technology (chuño) developed by Andean civilizations for oca and potato preservation represents one of the most sophisticated food preservation techniques in pre-industrial history.
modern_research.significance: Oca represents another example of the Andean food-medicine continuum. The chuño freeze-drying technique developed for oca and potato preservation is one of the most sophisticated food technologies in pre-industrial history — and created a reliable pharmaceutical supply chain for plant medicines in the Inca empire.

plants.evidence_authority: Anthocyanin anti-inflammatory documented; Vitamin C well-established; oxalic acid kidney stone risk at high doses.
plants.priority_reason: Andean food-medicine continuum · Anthocyanin anti-inflammatory documented · Chuño preservation technology historically significant

celtic_norse.source_text: Bald's Leechbook (c.900 CE); Lacnunga; Anglo-Saxon herbals
celtic_norse.original: Gearwe — wið wunda, wið blodrennan, wið fefor
celtic_norse.translation: Yarrow — for wounds, for bleeding, for fever. Bald's Leechbook documents yarrow extensively for wound healing and haemostasis. Also in the Nine Herbs Charm (Lacnunga). Used across Anglo-Saxon medicine for wound treatment, fever, and as an ingredient in compound preparations. The name connects to the Greek legend of Achilles using yarrow to stanch wounds on the battlefield — the genus Achillea named for this tradition.
celtic_norse.preparation: Fresh leaves applied directly to wounds; decocted for fever; compounded with other herbs
celtic_norse.condition: Wound bleeding, wound infection, fever, respiratory complaints

modern_research.key_compound: Chamazulene, achillin (sesquiterpene lactones), salicylic acid derivatives, flavonoids
modern_research.summary: Chamazulene: documented anti-inflammatory activity (same compound in chamomile). Achillin: antimicrobial. Salicylic acid derivatives: analgesic and antipyretic — validates traditional fever use. Hemostatic mechanism: promotes platelet aggregation and vasoconstriction. Documented antimicrobial activity against common wound pathogens.
modern_research.significance: Yarrow is the most widely documented medicinal plant in Moerman's Native American Ethnobotany Database — 46 independent nations. Combined with Celtic/Norse and Greek/Roman documentation, yarrow has among the strongest cross-tradition convergence for wound healing and fever in the archive. The hemostatic and anti-inflammatory mechanisms directly validate traditional wound applications.

plants.evidence_authority: Systematic reviews on wound healing; haemostatic mechanism documented; anti-inflammatory activity confirmed. Used in European herbal pharmacopoeia.
plants.priority_reason: 46 North American nations + Celtic/Norse + Greek/Roman convergence for wound healing · Hemostatic mechanism validated · Anti-inflammatory compounds confirmed · Represents archive's strongest global convergence for wound care

celtic_norse.source_text: Bald's Leechbook (c.900 CE); Nine Herbs Charm (Lacnunga) — listed separately from broadleaf plantain
celtic_norse.original: Wegbrade — wið hwosta, wið blode spiwunge
celtic_norse.translation: Ribwort (narrow-leaved plantain) — for cough, for spitting of blood. Anglo-Saxon medicine documents ribwort plantain as a specific for respiratory conditions — cough and respiratory bleeding — distinguished from the broader waybread (P. major) used for wounds. The distinction between the two Plantago species in Anglo-Saxon medicine is pharmacologically significant: P. lanceolata has higher concentration of mucilage and aucubin than P. major, making it more suitable for respiratory applications.
celtic_norse.preparation: Decocted for respiratory; fresh leaves applied to wounds

modern_research.summary: Aucubin: anti-inflammatory and antibronchospasm activity. Mucilage: soothing effect on irritated respiratory mucosa. Multiple studies confirming respiratory benefit of P. lanceolata. ESCOP and Commission E approve ribwort for respiratory catarrh. Clinical trial showing significant reduction in cough frequency.
modern_research.significance: Ribwort plantain's Anglo-Saxon use specifically for respiratory conditions (cough, haemoptysis) is pharmacologically validated by aucubin's anti-inflammatory and mucilage's soothing mechanisms. The Anglo-Saxon distinction between P. major (wound/digestive) and P. lanceolata (respiratory) reflects genuine pharmacological differences between the two species.

plants.evidence_authority: ESCOP and Commission E approved for respiratory catarrh; aucubin anti-inflammatory documented; clinical trials positive.

celtic_norse.original: Netele — wið wunda, wið naedran slite
celtic_norse.translation: Nettle — for wounds, for snake bite. Dead nettle (Lamium) documented alongside stinging nettle (Urtica) in Anglo-Saxon medicine. Red dead nettle specifically documented for wound healing and as an antiseptic application. Also used for respiratory conditions and female health.
celtic_norse.preparation: Leaves applied to wounds; decocted for internal use
celtic_norse.condition: Wound infection, respiratory complaints, menstrual conditions

modern_research.summary: White dead nettle (L. album) has documented anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic activity. European Pharmacopoeia includes L. album for leucorrhoea and menstrual conditions. Limited research on red dead nettle specifically. Tannins: astringent wound-healing activity consistent with traditional application.
modern_research.significance: Dead nettle's wound-healing application in Anglo-Saxon medicine is consistent with documented tannin astringency. The white dead nettle species has European Pharmacopoeia recognition for gynecological applications — validating the traditional menstrual use.

plants.evidence_authority: European Pharmacopoeia includes Lamium album for gynecological conditions; tannin astringency documented.
plants.priority_reason: Anglo-Saxon wound application consistent with tannin mechanism · L. album pharmacopoeia recognition · Limited research for red variety specifically

celtic_norse.source_text: Anglo-Saxon herbals — alternative name for pellitory
celtic_norse.original: Everthroat — wið toðece, wið heafodece
celtic_norse.translation: Everthroat (pellitory) — for toothache, for headache. An Anglo-Saxon name for pellitory, documented for the same dental and head pain applications as the Greek/Roman tradition. The name 'everthroat' refers to the burning sensation produced when the root is chewed — the same property that makes it effective for dental pain.
celtic_norse.preparation: Root chewed for dental pain; applied topically

modern_research.summary: Pellitorine: alkamide producing numbing/tingling sensation via TRPV1/TRPA1 receptor activation. This directly validates the traditional dental pain application — the same class of compounds in Echinacea and the toothache plant (Spilanthes).
modern_research.significance: See MMA-069 (Pellitory). This entry documents the same plant under its Anglo-Saxon name 'Everthroat' — the burning throat sensation of the root chewed is the same alkamide mechanism validated for dental analgesia.

plants.evidence_authority: Pellitorine numbness mechanism documented; alkamide pharmacology studied; no approved drug.
plants.priority_reason: Duplicate documentation of MMA-069 under Anglo-Saxon name · Alkamide numbing mechanism validated

celtic_norse.original: Hune — wið þone þe blodrenne, wið þone innyflan oman
celtic_norse.translation: Horehound — for blood-running (haemoptysis), for internal erysipelas. Bald's Leechbook documents horehound for respiratory conditions including cough and blood-spitting. Also used for digestive complaints and fever. One of the most consistently mentioned plants in Anglo-Saxon medicine for respiratory conditions.
celtic_norse.preparation: Decocted in water or ale; taken in honey for respiratory

modern_research.summary: Marrubiin: potent expectorant and antispasmodic activity on bronchial smooth muscle. FDA approved horehound as an OTC cough suppressant ingredient until 1989 when reformulation of OTC drug categories was required (lack of current data, not safety concerns). European Commission E approves horehound for cough. Multiple pharmacological studies confirming expectorant mechanism.
modern_research.significance: Horehound for cough was validated enough to receive FDA OTC approval before regulatory changes required new data submissions. The Anglo-Saxon documentation for respiratory conditions is directly validated by marrubiin's expectorant mechanism.

plants.evidence_authority: FDA formerly approved as OTC cough suppressant ingredient; EU Commission E approved for cough; marrubiin expectorant mechanism well-documented.

tradition_names.celtic_norse: Pipor (Old English — black pepper traded into Anglo-Saxon England)

celtic_norse.source_text: Bald's Leechbook (c.900 CE) — pepper appears in multiple compound preparations
celtic_norse.original: Pipor — in manegum lacnungum. Wið cyle, wið fefor, wið innonfærld.
celtic_norse.translation: Pepper — in many remedies. For chills, for fever, for internal ailments. Bald's Leechbook includes pepper in multiple compound preparations — primarily as a warming ingredient for digestive and respiratory complaints. Archaeological finds confirm pepper was traded to Anglo-Saxon England despite its distant origin, demonstrating how highly valued it was as a medicinal ingredient.
celtic_norse.preparation: Ground and mixed in ale or wine; added to compound preparations
celtic_norse.condition: Chills, fever, digestive warming, respiratory conditions

modern_research.key_compound: Piperine (5-9% by weight), piperic acid, essential oils
modern_research.summary: See MMA-052 (Black Pepper) for full research summary. This entry documents the Anglo-Saxon tradition's adoption of black pepper as a key medicinal ingredient — adding a third documentation context (alongside Dioscorides' Greek/Roman and Cobo's Andean documentation).
modern_research.significance: Anglo-Saxon use of pepper as a warming and digestive medicine is pharmacologically consistent with piperine's TRPV1 activation (heat receptor) and bioavailability-enhancing properties. The fact that pepper was traded to distant Anglo-Saxon England despite its cost demonstrates the high value placed on it as a medicine.

plants.evidence_authority: See MMA-052. Piperine TRPV1 activation documented; bioavailability enhancement well-established; GRAS.
plants.priority_reason: Third independent documentation for pepper · Piperine bioavailability mechanism suggests value in combination formulations
